docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Union Mechs
docs.text:
Union mech forces combine widely available Core designs with heavy industrial frames built for mass deployment, durability, and sustained mechanized assault.

Mech Model Raptor RPT-1R Vagabond VG-7H Slugger SLG-1A Alpha Wolf AWLF-5K Crossfire CRF-0S Firebrand FR-3N Dragoon DGN-8S Maverick MAV-8K Jackman JCK-77 Patriot PTR-007 Hammerhead HMR-4T Challenger CHL-4R Brigadier BRG-88R Bulwark BLW-5K Sword Breaker SBRK-7R Punisher PNR-004 Gallimagus GLM-8G Liberator LBR-9D

docs.title: Raptor RPT-1R
docs.text: Developed as a fast-response raider during the Union’s early frontier wars, the Raptor sacrifices heavy armor for speed and strike capability. Its rocket pods and light lasers made it ideal for harassing supply lines and crippling softer targets before heavier Union assets arrived. Though lightly protected, pilots trusted its agility and ferocity—earning the name Raptor for its swift, predatory strikes.

docs.title: Vagabond VG-7H
docs.text: The Vagabond was born of necessity during the Exile, when scattered Union units required a versatile scout and skirmisher. Outfitted with jump jets and a mixed ballistic-energy loadout, it excels in rough terrain where heavier mechs cannot tread. Its name reflects both its wandering origins and its reputation as a survivor, roaming far from supply lines and returning with kills to prove its worth.

docs.title: Slugger SLG-1A
docs.text: Designed for brutal city fighting during the Restoration, the Slugger carries a heavy energy cannon backed by close-support lasers. Slow but unyielding, it was built to clear enemy strongpoints building by building. The name Slugger is both a nod to its hammering firepower and the Union’s reputation for winning through attrition, no matter how long the fight drags on.

docs.title: Alpha Wolf AWLF-5K
docs.text: The Alpha Wolf represents the Union’s doctrine of coordinated pack warfare. Armed with paired autoguns, lasers, and missiles, it was designed to anchor wolfpack formations that overwhelmed isolated foes through relentless firepower. Feared for its ability to tear through both armor and morale, the Alpha Wolf earned its name by always leading the kill.

docs.title: Crossfire CRF-0S
docs.text: Born from Union doctrine that no enemy should leave the field unscathed, the Crossfire bristles with overlapping weapon arcs—Vulkan cannon, rockets, lasers, and autogun. It was intended for siege and suppression, saturating kill zones until nothing remained. Pilots often joked that standing in its line of fire was like being caught between two armies—hence the name Crossfire.

docs.title: Firebrand FR-3N
docs.text: The Firebrand’s origin lies in the Union’s scorched-earth campaigns. Armed with an array of Scorchers supported by lasers and autoguns, it was built to terrorize entrenched enemies, burning them from cover and leaving nothing but ash. The Union embraced the mech’s fiery symbolism, fielding it as both weapon and warning: resistance would be answered with flames.

docs.title: Dragoon DGN-8S
docs.text: The Dragoon was designed as a flexible heavy line unit, able to hold or advance with equal confidence. Combining a heavy energy cannon, vulkan, and defensive lasers, it proved especially valuable in prolonged campaigns where reliability outweighed elegance. Its name recalls the mounted infantry of old—soldiers who could fight wherever they were needed, a tradition the Union holds dear.

docs.title: Maverick MAV-8K
docs.text: The Maverick was developed for commanders who demanded independence from supply trains. Its rockets and high energy cannon made it a devastating ambush platform, while its jump jets allowed it to strike from unexpected angles. The name Maverick honors both its battlefield role and the defiant Union spirit of breaking from convention to seize victory.

docs.title: Jackman JCK-77
docs.text: The Jackman was built during the urban reclamations, where Union forces needed a close-range brawler to clear streets and hold chokepoints. Armed with paired vulkans and heavy lasers, it is devastating in knife-fight ranges. The name reflects the laborers who “jackhammered” the Union’s cities back into shape, embodying relentless industrial grit turned to war.

docs.title: Patriot PTR-007
docs.text: The Patriot is perhaps the most symbolic of Union mechs, a walking fortress embodying their doctrine of sacrifice and resilience. With thick armor, broad weapon coverage, and missile support, it became the backbone of ceremonial as well as battlefield formations. Its name was no accident—the Union intended it as a rallying banner, a machine that stood unbroken while others fell.

docs.title: Hammerhead HMR-4T
docs.text: Developed for shipboard and port assaults, the Hammerhead mounts twin heavy energy cannons backed by autoguns and lasers. Its name derives from its brutal frontal assault style, “hammering” through defenses like the predator of the sea. Pilots often describe it as inelegant but unstoppable—exactly the qualities the Union prizes.

docs.title: Challenger CHL-4R
docs.text: The Challenger was created during the Union’s dueling trials, where mechs were fielded to prove dominance over rival Houses. Mixing rockets, autoguns, and lasers, it was designed for versatility in single combat as well as battlefield challenges. Though less specialized than some designs, its name and legacy are steeped in Union pride: to challenge is to overcome.

docs.title: Brigadier BRG-88R
docs.text: Built to lead batteries of missile carriers, the Brigadier became the cornerstone of Union siege doctrine. Capable of drowning entrenched foes in waves of rockets and missiles, it earned a reputation for ending battles before they truly began. The name Brigadier reflects its command role—always at the center of Union formations, directing fire and breaking lines.

docs.title: Bulwark BLW-5K
docs.text: Designed as a defensive anchor, the Bulwark carries massive armor and layered weaponry suited for holding ground against superior numbers. In campaigns where the Union was forced to defend its hard-won territories, the Bulwark became both shield and hammer. Its name speaks for itself: immovable, unshakable, a wall of steel.

docs.title: Sword Breaker SBRK-7R
docs.text: The Sword Breaker was engineered specifically to counter the dueling champions of rival Houses. With heavy vulkans and precision medium lasers, it excelled at stripping weapons from enemy mechs and leaving them powerless. Its name is both literal and symbolic—the Union’s message that no foreign blade, however sharp, could pierce their resolve.

docs.title: Punisher PNR-004
docs.text: The Punisher was born from campaigns of retribution. With a heavy energy cannon, ultra vulkan, and missile support, it was built to chase down and annihilate those who dared defy Union authority. Slow but merciless, the Punisher lives up to its name, serving as executioner on the battlefield where no mercy is given.

docs.title: Gallimagus GLM-8G
docs.text: The Gallimagus is an ancient design, said to descend from pre-Exile Empire prototypes. Mounting an unprecedented four heavy energy cannons, it was rarely produced due to cost, but every unit became a battlefield legend. Its name, drawn from old Union sagas, evokes a monstrous beast of war—rare, mythical, and terrifying in its wrath.

docs.title: Liberator LBR-9D
docs.text: Developed during the Long Exile by remnants of the fallen Empire, the Liberator was designed to spearhead counteroffensives and break enemy sieges. It played a decisive role in the Great Restoration, leading the charge to reclaim the Core worlds. Adopted as the Union’s premiere assault mech, it endures as both battlefield powerhouse and enduring symbol of liberation.

docs.title: Sovereignty Mechs
docs.text:
Sovereignty mech forces combine common Core platforms with prestigious, high-quality designs associated with aristocratic command, battlefield coordination, and visible military refinement.

Mech Model Messenger MSG-1R Icarus ICS-3G Pegasus PGS-H Sentry SN-21 Warden WRD-N1 Lamplighter LLT-6Y Legionnaire LGN-A Paladin PLDN-00C Herald HRL-6D Protector PRO-T1 Dark Knight DK-2R Excalibur EX-3R Lionheart LHR-5T Lancelot LNC-10T Great Sword SWRD-001 Colossus COL-U5 Monarch MON-A7 Olympian OLYM-D

docs.title: Messenger MSG-1R
docs.text: A swift harasser and scout, the Messenger relies on jump jets and missile bursts to unsettle lighter foes. Its role is to deliver a clear message on the battlefield—that Sovereignty forces are advancing, and their arrival is inevitable.

docs.title: Icarus ICS-3G
docs.text: The Icarus embodies Sovereignty daring and hubris—fast, jump-capable, and armed with rockets for dramatic strikes. Though lightly armored, its theatrics earned it glory in countless tournaments. Pilots know the cautionary tale of its name, but many still fly it recklessly, chasing the sun of renown.

docs.title: Pegasus PGS-H
docs.text: Inspired by myth, the Pegasus was designed as an aerial duelist, its jump jets giving it a graceful leap few others could match. Bristling with light and medium lasers, it dances around heavier foes, striking with elegance. In parades as in battle, the Pegasus is prized as a symbol of Sovereignty’s radiant majesty.

docs.title: Sentry SN-21
docs.text: The Sentry was built for watchful dominance, its long-range high energy cannon and missile banks intended to hold borders in clear view. It lacks mobility, but its very presence is a deterrent, projecting Sovereignty vigilance. Many noble estates still keep a Sentry posted at their gates, a statue of steel as much as a guardian.

docs.title: Warden WRD-N1
docs.text: Where the Sentry watches, the Warden intervenes. Agile for its size, it was built to guard noble households and convoy routes. Its heavy laser and missiles strike with authority, while its name reinforces Sovereignty propaganda: every subject is safe under noble protection—so long as they remain loyal.

docs.title: Lamplighter LLT-6Y
docs.text: A mech named for illumination, the Lamplighter was created to pierce darkness both literal and symbolic. Its long-range energy cannon and array of lasers allow it to light up a battlefield from afar. To the Sovereignty, deploying Lamplighters is an act of enlightenment, burning away shadows of rebellion with radiant fire.

docs.title: Legionnaire LGN-A
docs.text: The Legionnaire was mass-produced during Sovereignty campaigns to enforce order in the Core. Fitted with a medium MAC, missiles, and lasers, it is a versatile soldier’s machine. The name recalls the legions of ancient empires—disciplined, unstoppable, and loyal to the Sovereignty above all else.

docs.title: Paladin PLDN-00C
docs.text: A mech wrapped in religious and chivalric symbolism, the Paladin was designed to embody the Sovereignty’s holy mandate. Rockets for righteous fury, lasers for guiding light, and a MAC to deliver judgment. In battle, it is as much a sermon as a weapon, reminding allies and enemies alike of the Sovereignty’s divine right to rule.

docs.title: Herald HRL-6D
docs.text: Where nobles feared assassination or insurrection, Heralds were deployed. Heavy armor and powerful missile barrages made them excellent protectors of VIPs and sacred sites. To this day, Sovereignty rulers keep Heralds in their personal retinues, their name a constant reminder of paternal care—and veiled threat.

docs.title: Protector PRO-T1
docs.text: The Protector was created as a battlefield shield for Sovereignty banners. Mixing heavy lasers with rockets and missiles, it can blunt enemy advances while rallying allies. Noble houses often decorate Protectors with gilded heraldry, ensuring that their role as both bulwark and symbol is unmistakable.

docs.title: Dark Knight DK-2R
docs.text: The Dark Knight carries an aura of dread deliberately cultivated by Sovereignty engineers. Long-range energy weapons paired with a heavy laser give it punishing reach, while its sinister name and black-lacquered armor turn it into an icon of fear. Where the Dark Knight walks, rebellion tends to wither.

docs.title: Excalibur EX-3R
docs.text: Few names carry more weight than Excalibur, and the Sovereignty bestowed it upon this duelist’s chassis. Armed with MACs, lasers, and rockets, it was designed for decisive, theatrical victories. Excaliburs often lead tournament fields, their pilots draped in noble colors, striking as much for honor as for conquest.

docs.title: Lionheart LHR-5T
docs.text: The Lionheart epitomizes Sovereignty nobility: powerful, courageous, and unflinching. With heavy lasers, rockets, and long-range cannons, it dominates battlefields while bearing crests of golden lions. More than a weapon, it is a living coat of arms, proof that Sovereignty bloodlines rule by strength and valor.

docs.title: Lancelot LNC-10T
docs.text: The Lancelot was built to combine elegance with battlefield dominance. Its MAC and missile systems give it both reach and crushing power, while its lasers keep foes at bay. No Sovereignty parade is complete without a Lancelot striding at the fore, the paragon of chivalric warfare.

docs.title: Great Sword SWRD-001
docs.text: The Great Sword is the Sovereignty’s answer to the Union’s brute heavies: less subtle, more theatrical. Quad MACs and paired heavy lasers strike with the force of a descending blade. Its very silhouette evokes a weapon raised for the killing blow, a message that Sovereignty justice is swift and merciless.

docs.title: Colossus COL-U5
docs.text: Massive and imposing, the Colossus is a walking fortress designed to awe as much as destroy. Mounting a heavy MAC alongside multiple lasers, it crushes resistance under overwhelming firepower. Wherever a Colossus is deployed, it is as much a proclamation as an army—Sovereignty power manifest in steel.

docs.title: Monarch MON-A7
docs.text: The Monarch was never just a war machine—it was designed as a throne of iron for Sovereignty lords. With missile arrays and heavy lasers, it wields devastating power while carrying the regalia of its house. To see a Monarch on the field is to witness Sovereignty authority embodied, its very name leaving no doubt of who rules.

docs.title: Olympian OLYM-D
docs.text: The Olympian is the pinnacle of Sovereignty design philosophy: mythic in name, majestic in presence, and terrible in power. With MACs, long-range cannons, missiles, and lasers, it commands every battlefield range. Sovereignty propaganda hails it as proof of their divine heritage—an Olympian god made metal, standing above all mortal challengers.

docs.title: Protectorate Mechs
docs.text:
Protectorate mech forces combine shared Core workhorses with rugged heavy machines designed for endurance, harsh-environment combat, and holding defensive lines under extreme pressure.

Mech Model War Dog WAR-D1 Reaver RVR-N5 Jackal JCK-L4 Harbinger HAR-G9 Beast BST-R3 Instigator INS-G9 Razorback RZK-7B Neanderthal NEA-R8 Barbarian BAR-N8 Overlord OVR-L1 Berserker BER-Z3 Thunder God TDR-G7 Infernal INF-L9 Doomhammer DOOM-H2 Brute Machine BRT-100R Cromagnon CRM-N2 Juggernaut JGR-N6 Ragnarok RGK-5000K

docs.title: War Dog WAR-D1
docs.text: One of the oldest fielded designs in the Protectorate arsenal, the War Dog earned its name in decades of border wars where packs of them ran down raiders under cover of smoke. Reliable and loyal in function, it remains a cornerstone of Protectorate doctrine.

docs.title: Reaver RVR-N5
docs.text: First deployed during the Black Hills Campaign, the Reaver became infamous for tearing through lighter mechs with its crushing fist and rockets. Its brutal reputation lingers; to Protectorate troops, a Reaver on the field is a promise of broken lines.

docs.title: Jackal JCK-L4
docs.text: Known as the “stormcrow” of the Protectorate, the Harbinger often appears at the outset of offensives, signaling devastation to come. Its combination of fists, vulkan, and lasers makes it a herald of annihilation as much as a front-line bruiser.

docs.title: Harbinger HAR-G9
docs.text: Named for its role as a herald of destruction, the Harbinger combines brute force with relentless suppressive fire. Its battle fist and vulkan cannon hammer enemies at close range, while laser support ensures there is no safe angle of approach.

docs.title: Beast BST-R3
docs.text: The Beast traces back to Protectorate shock-divisions raised from penal battalions. Pilots dubbed it for its savage ultra-vulkan recoil and feral silhouette, and the name stuck. Today it remains a terror weapon, more feared than refined.

docs.title: Instigator INS-G9
docs.text: The Instigator is a line-breaker, built to provoke chaos. Its heavy cannon and rocket payload drive enemies from cover, while laser fire harasses stragglers. True to its name, it starts fights the Protectorate intends to finish.

docs.title: Razorback RZK-7B
docs.text: With its distinctive armored ridges, the Razorback resembles the boar for which it is named. It first entered service during the Dust Marches, where its heavy autogun and laser battery carved corridors through smoke-choked canyons.

docs.title: Neanderthal NEA-R8
docs.text: The Neanderthal earned its name during testing, where its sheer, blunt brutality demolished mock fortresses in minutes. It remains the Protectorate’s premier siege breaker, a mech that embodies unrefined but devastating power.

docs.title: Barbarian BAR-N8
docs.text: The Barbarian embodies the Protectorate’s doctrine of raw shock power. With its crushing fist, heavy lasers, and layered autoguns, it thrives in brutal close-quarters fighting where finesse is meaningless and only overwhelming force decides the outcome.

docs.title: Overlord OVR-L1
docs.text: The Overlord was created as a command mech for Protectorate strike groups, its size and armament meant to dominate attention. Though later fielded in greater numbers, its reputation as a battlefield overseer still clings to it.

docs.title: Berserker BER-Z3
docs.text: The Berserker channels raw ferocity into every engagement. Its crushing fist and banks of medium lasers let it drive headlong into enemy lines, while smoke rounds shroud its reckless advance. It is feared less for precision than for the chaos it unleashes up close.

docs.title: Thunder God TDR-G7
docs.text: The Thunder God takes its name from its battlefield presence—heavy cannons and vulkans that echo like storms breaking over the horizon. Protectorate lore holds that its arrival signals the final hammerblow of a campaign.

docs.title: Infernal INF-L9
docs.text: The Infernal’s earliest deployments left entire valleys scorched black, and its fire-spewing design cemented its apocalyptic reputation. Protectorate commanders often use it as a psychological weapon as much as a physical one.

docs.title: Doomhammer DOOM-H2
docs.text: The Doomhammer was first forged to break stone bunkers and hardened enemy positions. With massive vulkans and crushing fists, it still fulfills that grim role, delivering hammerblows of smoke and fire to erase dug-in resistance.

docs.title: Brute Machine BRT-100R
docs.text: A product of rushed wartime design, the Brute Machine was derided for its inelegance but proved itself by sheer weight of fire. Its survival and adoption are proof of the Protectorate’s core belief: overwhelming force wins wars.

docs.title: Cromagnon CRM-N2
docs.text: The Cromagnon’s name came from rival houses mocking its primitive simplicity—until Protectorate pilots smashed through their most advanced fortifications. Now it is worn with pride, a blunt instrument that proves sophistication isn’t always required.

docs.title: Juggernaut JGR-N6
docs.text: The second heaviest mech ever fielded in the Core, the Juggernaut was first deployed to break the sieges of the Border Wars, where its unstoppable advance became legend. With layered armor, a crushing fist, and a mix of rockets, lasers, and autoguns, it grinds forward until resistance collapses. Entire fortresses have fallen simply by being in its path.

docs.title: Ragnarok RGK-5000K
docs.text: The largest mech ever fielded, the Ragnarok is the end made metal. A terrifying opponent in close ranges, it is heavily armed with vulkans, cannons, and scorcher systems and turns battlefields into apocalyptic wastelands. Wherever it marches, smoke and ruin follow, sealing the fate of all who oppose the Protectorate.

docs.title: Imperium Mechs
docs.text:
Imperium mech forces combine common Core platforms with fast, elegant, often serpentine machines built around mobility, opening volleys, and the doctrine of fierce beauty.

Mech Model Zephyr ZPH-01 Drake DRK-06 Ronin RON-12 Pyrefox PYX-53 Incursor ICR-99 Jaguar JGR-72 Ocelot OCE-45 Umbrantarch UMB-39 Redeemer RDM-10 Nagara NGA-47 Drakkon DRK-82 Ordosmech ORD-07 Warclaw WCL-61 Nightwing NGT-W4 Onagor ONA-37 Praetorian PRA-97 Neomech NEO-60 Star Slayer SLY-10

docs.title: Zephyr ZPH-01
docs.text: A relic of the Imperium’s dueling traditions, the Zephyr was designed for blinding speed, able to outpace heavier mechs with ease. Armed with a blaster and light lasers, it darts in, strikes, and vanishes, leaving foes bewildered. Its name speaks to its nature: swift as wind, impossible to grasp.

docs.title: Drake DRK-06
docs.text: The Drake was crafted in homage to the Imperium’s dragon symbology, its blaster and twin scorchers breathing fire across enemy lines. Lightly armored but terrifying up close, it thrives in ambushes and raids. Pilots often paint their machines with scaled patterns, reinforcing its image as a dragon reborn in steel.

docs.title: Ronin RON-12
docs.text: A mech for warriors without masters, the Ronin was adopted by Imperium mercenary bands during the Old Imperium. With rockets for sudden fury and a blaster for dueling, it embodies the lone fighter’s creed: self-reliance, defiance, and a readiness to sell its blade to any cause.

docs.title: Pyrefox PYX-53
docs.text: True to its name, the Pyrefox engulfs battlefields with blasters and fire weapons, making it a terror in close combat. Developed to cleanse rebellious enclaves, it carries both symbolic and practical weight—reminding enemies that resisting the Imperium will always end in flames.

docs.title: Incursor ICR-99
docs.text: A reckless raider’s machine, the Incursor is fast, lightly protected, and dangerously over-armed for its frame. Rockets, blasters, and lasers make it unpredictable and deadly in the hands of bold pilots. Many Imperium aces built their reputations in Incursors, treating each battle as both gamble and performance.

docs.title: Jaguar JGR-72
docs.text: Named for the fierce predator, the Jaguar embodies speed and ferocity. Its vulkan cannon and blaster combination allows it to tear through lighter foes, while its design prioritizes the swift, lethal strike over defense. In Imperium culture, Jaguars are seen as hunters born for war, and this mech carries that tradition.

docs.title: Ocelot OCE-45
docs.text: The Ocelot was built to haunt enemies at range, its heavy energy cannon and blasters striking before foes can retaliate. Though lightly armored, its eerie precision and sudden appearances earned it a reputation as a phantom of the battlefield. Among Imperium warriors, piloting a Ocelot is considered an honor tied to ancestral rites of death and remembrance.

docs.title: Umbrantarch UMB-39
docs.text: The Umbrantarch is a fast, fear-inducing strike mech armed with a heavy blaster and scorcher systems, optimized for repeated hit-and-run assaults. More than a combat platform, the Umbrantarch is regarded as a terror weapon—its silhouette emerging through fire and smoke like an omen. Umbrantarch pilots are said to walk with spirits, feared as much by allies as by enemies.

docs.title: Redeemer RDM-10
docs.text: The Redeemer is a fast, hard-hitting medium platform designed for decisive strikes against exposed targets. Its rockets and blasters allow it to deliver sudden, concentrated fire before repositioning. Redeemers are deployed where momentum must be seized quickly and held through force.

docs.title: Nagara NGA-47
docs.text: Built during an era of holy wars, the Nagara combined heavy firepower with symbolic weight. With its HEC and heavy Vulkan cannon, it was designed to break enemy champions and restore order. To this day, the Imperium frames Nagaras as machines of salvation through fire and steel.

docs.title: Drakkon DRK-82
docs.text: A monstrous war machine, the Drakkon is drenched in flamethrowers and blasters, spewing destruction in every direction. It earned its name not from myth, but from its battlefield presence—unyielding, terrifying, and impossible to ignore. To the Imperium, the Drakkon is both dragon and executioner.

docs.title: Ordosmech ORD-07
docs.text: The Ordosmech embodies the Imperium’s martial faith, built as a crusader’s machine. Armed with Vulkan cannons, rockets, and blasters, it balances mass firepower with religious symbolism. Pilots clad their Ordosmechs in heraldry and ritual markings, treating each battle as both ritual war and ancestral duty.

docs.title: Warclaw WCL-61
docs.text: The Warclaw was commissioned during the Imperium’s early expansion campaigns, when rapid assaults proved insufficient against fortified resistance. Designed for prolonged engagements, it combines blasters, Vulkan cannons, and scorcher systems to apply constant pressure while advancing methodically. The Warclaw is deployed when territory must be taken and held, not merely breached.

docs.title: Nightwing NGT-W4
docs.text: The Nightwing prowls battlefields like a shadow, unleashing rockets, flamethrowers, and blaster fire before fading back into darkness. Heavily armed for its size, it embodies the Imperium’s preference for intimidation as well as annihilation. Its name, like its silhouette, evokes wings spread across the night sky.

docs.title: Onagor ONA-37
docs.text: The Onagor was built as a shock unit, carrying heavy vulkans and blasters to overwhelm enemies with sheer volume of fire. Though slower than some peers, it compensates with raw, thunderous destruction. Onagor is an ancient Imperium name, drawn from pre-unification myths describing a colossal entity whose advance was said to end wars by erasing the ground they were fought on.

docs.title: Praetorian PRA-97
docs.text: The Praetorian is one of the Imperium’s most feared and recognizable war machines. With paired heavy energy cannons, blasters, and scorchers, it combines versatile firepower with an imposing silhouette that dominates the field. More than a frontline brawler, the Praetorian is a commander’s mech, deployed to anchor formations and inspire dread in equal measure.

docs.title: Neomech NEO-60
docs.text: Born of experiments in massed rocket fire, the Neomech is a heavy line unit that drowns enemies in salvos while its blasters and lasers cut down survivors. Its name reflects both its modernity and its connection to the Imperium’s constant cycle of rebirth: old forms reforged into new engines of war.

docs.title: Star Slayer SLY-10
docs.text: The Star Slayer represents the Imperium’s mythic ambition. With heavy energy cannons and blasters, it was designed as a “god-killer,” capable of standing against the mightiest machines of rival Houses. Its name is both boast and prophecy—declaring that no star, no throne, and no rival shines brighter than the Imperium.

docs.title: Orion Mechs
docs.text:
Orion mech forces combine standard Core platforms with technologically advanced machines emphasizing sensors, precision, experimental systems, and battlefield efficiency.

Mech Model Photon PHN-50T Tachyon TCY-2R Zeno ZEN-400P Darius DR-300S Cobalt CLT-B1 Watcher WAT-002 Deviance DEV-2N Xerxes XER-200A Echelon ECH-4R Blaze Bringer BLZ-360K Reckoner REK-4M Eradicator ERAD-120R Zealot ZEL-20T Endermech END-550M Markov MKV-60L Rift Master RFM-220C Nebuchadnezzar NEB-Z001 Aeon AE-550X

docs.title: Photon PHN-50T
docs.text: Developed by the Sentinel Program under the Orion Standards Commission, the Photon was the first widely fielded scout with projected shielding. Its medium prismatic array doubles as a sensor-liaison tool, letting it tag targets and survive brushes that would scrap other lights.

docs.title: Tachyon TCY-2R
docs.text: An offshoot of the Photon II trials, the Tachyon prioritized velocity and precision fire for counter-raider screens. Twin light and one medium prismatic give it sharp, repeatable bursts, while its avionics tie directly into early-warning buoys. On border nets, “tachyon alert” means a fast mover has already cut the angle.

docs.title: Zeno ZEN-400P
docs.text: Created for medium-range interdiction, the Zeno pairs a medium prismatic with economical rocket salvos to wear down fleeing targets. Modular hardpoints and easy field service made it a favorite of Citizen Guard patrols. Its name nods to pursuit paradoxes—once a Zeno starts the chase, the gap never seems to close for the prey.

docs.title: Darius DR-300S
docs.text: Commissioned by the Heavy Systems Bureau to give citizen brigades a dependable lineholder, the Darius blends twin medium prismatics with stabilizing shields and guided missiles. It lacks flourish but excels at steady positional warfare, advancing behind a rational wall of data and fire. Crews respect it for what it is: the dependable center of an orderly advance.

docs.title: Cobalt CLT-B1
docs.text: A precision striker from the Alloy Labs Initiative, the Cobalt mates a heavy energy cannon to a medium prismatic under reinforced shields. Its distinctive blue ceramic laminate isn’t for show—it dissipates heat and mitigates spall. Graduation units from several academies award “Cobalt keys” to pilots certified on its demanding fire-control regimen.

docs.title: Watcher WAT-002
docs.text: Designed as a mesh-linked sentry, the Watcher stitches missiles, rockets, and prismatics into the battlespace network, shaping enemy movement rather than chasing it. When a Watcher is present, commanders talk about lanes and closures—the field becomes a diagram, and the diagram favors Orion.

docs.title: Deviance DEV-2N
docs.text: The Deviance began as an unsanctioned field refit, where pilots overloaded a standard chassis with heavy prismatic arrays and rockets beyond recommended tolerances. Though the Assembly initially condemned the design, its brutal effectiveness in trials forced acceptance. Its name stuck, a reminder that sometimes progress comes from breaking rules.

docs.title: Xerxes XER-200A
docs.text: Part of the Archives’ “Kings Series” naming convention, the Xerxes is an expeditionary spearhead with twin heavy prismatics, rockets, and resilient shields. It isn’t subtle—its job is to impose order at the point of contact. Citizen brigades treat it like a mobile verdict: the argument ends where Xerxes stands.

docs.title: Echelon ECH-4R
docs.text: Built for charter enforcement and clean disables, the Echelon uses paired light prismatics and mixed missiles to peel weapons without catastrophic kills. Its telemetry suite prioritizes limb and mount solutions over cores. Echelons are routinely assigned to interdiction details, where compliance is required intact rather than destroyed.

docs.title: Blaze Bringer BLZ-360K
docs.text: A field-illumination and suppression platform, the Blaze Bringer throws a curtain of coherent light—medium and light prismatics that dazzle sensors and scour cover. High sink capacity lets it sustain the show long after others cool off. Night operations doctrine calls it “turning darkness to glass.”

docs.title: Reckoner REK-4M
docs.text: The Reckoner serves as an anchor and command relay. Heavy and medium prismatics handle threat nodes while a rocket bank shapes approaches. Its hardened comms and fire-control links allow it to coordinate allied units under sustained contact. Once established, a Reckoner position is intended to be held, not redeployed.

docs.title: Eradicator ERAD-120R
docs.text: Despite the severe name, the Eradicator is a surgical siege tool. A heavy energy cannon and twin prismatics cut precise breach patterns while rockets clear debris and counter-pushes. City engineers like working behind it; the hole it makes is the hole the plan expected.

docs.title: Zealot ZEL-20T
docs.text: Adopted enthusiastically by Citizen Guard auxiliaries, the Zealot layers shields and a full prismatic suite to hold ground with conviction. Rockets punish flanking attempts while heavy and medium beams keep pressure steady. Its “zeal” isn’t mysticism—just the civic habit of not giving an inch.

docs.title: Endermech END-550M
docs.text: The Endermech serves as a versatile line-breaker, protected by advanced shielding. Armed with a heavy energy cannon, twin heavy prismatics, and missile support, it can adapt to almost any battlefield role. Pilots often remark on its uncanny precision, as if the machine anticipates threats a moment before they emerge.

docs.title: Markov MKV-60L
docs.text: The Markov integrates predictive targeting from the Markov Kernel Project with twin heavy prismatics and deep heat reserves. Pilots joke it only needs to lock once; after that the future is already computed. In analytics drills, its variance charts are a thing of austere beauty.

docs.title: Rift Master RFM-220C
docs.text: The Rift Master bends battlefields to its will. A chokepoint dominator, it seals narrow passes with heavy prismatic fire and rocket storms. Its shield lattice is tuned for glancing shots from extreme angles—perfect for canyon walls and megastructure corridors.

docs.title: Nebuchadnezzar NEB-Z001
docs.text: A prestige fortress platform from the Archives’ “City-Builder” lineage, the Nebuchadnezzar layers heavy, medium, and light prismatics with compliant missiles under deep shields. It’s meant to hold capitals and corridors, projecting calm inevitability. The name fits: it builds order by unbuilding resistance.

docs.title: Aeon AE-550X
docs.text: The Aeon is the pinnacle of Corporate technology, a mech designed to embody timeless supremacy. Quad heavy prismatics gave it unrivaled firepower, while advanced shields can make it feel invincible. Corporate propaganda has declared it ‘the machine that will outlast eternity.’

docs.title: Mechs
docs.text: “The first mech did not simply change warfare. It changed civilization.” — StarCom military archive

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Common Designation War Mech Typical Height 4–5 Stories Typical Weight 200–1200 Tons Primary Power Source Compact Fusion Core Control System Neural Interface Link Military Importance Dominant Ground Warfare Platform

War Mechs — commonly referred to simply as Mechs — are giant armored war machines used throughout the Core and frontier alike.

Towering several stories above the battlefield, mechs serve as the primary backbone of modern ground warfare.

Every major power in the Core maintains extensive mech forces, including: - the Great Houses - mercenary companies - frontier militias - corporate security divisions - Guild-chartered forces

Modern mechs vary enormously in: - size - armor - speed - weapons systems - battlefield doctrine

but most stand between four and five stories tall and weigh several hundred tons.

Despite their immense scale, advanced neural interfaces and compact fusion technology allow mechs to maneuver with surprising speed and precision.

Throughout civilized space, mechs are viewed simultaneously as: - weapons of war - symbols of political power - industrial marvels - cultural icons

For many citizens of the Core, the silhouette of a mech standing watch over a city skyline represents civilization itself.

docs.title: Mech Registries
docs.text:
• Mechs of the Core

The following registries catalog the major mech chassis used throughout the Core Worlds, including both common mech platforms and faction-specific war machines.

docs.title: Why Mechs Dominate Warfare
docs.text:
Modern military doctrine throughout the Core is built around mech warfare.

Although tanks, aerospace craft, infantry, artillery, and orbital fleets remain critically important, mechs occupy a uniquely flexible role unmatched by any other battlefield platform.

Mechs possess the ability to: - traverse difficult terrain - operate independently for extended periods - engage effectively in urban environments - wield modular weapons systems - survive catastrophic battlefield damage - rapidly adapt to changing combat conditions

Unlike traditional armored vehicles, mechs can: - climb unstable terrain - maneuver through dense cities - cross damaged infrastructure - operate in wilderness regions - engage enemies vertically as well as horizontally

The development of compact fusion cores also allows mechs to field extremely energy-intensive weapons systems while maintaining exceptional operational endurance.

In many frontier regions, a single mech lance can project more effective military force than entire conventional armored formations.

docs.title: History and Origins
docs.text:
The origins of mech technology trace back several centuries before the rise of the Old Empire.

The earliest predecessors to modern mechs included: - powered industrial exoskeletons - mechanized cargo loaders - primitive armored walkers - mobile defensive pillboxes

These early designs were cumbersome, unstable, and tactically limited.

Most lacked: - mobility - battlefield endurance - effective power systems - coordinated control mechanisms

As a result, early mechanized warfare remained impractical for large-scale military use.

docs.title: The Imperium Revolution
docs.text:
The transformative leap in mech technology came from the Omnisphere Imperium during the era before the rise of the Old Empire.

At the time, the Imperium controlled only a modest cluster of worlds.

Its engineers, however, pioneered several technological breakthroughs that would permanently reshape civilization.

These advancements included:

docs.title: Advanced Alloy Engineering
docs.text:
Imperium researchers developed lightweight ultra-durable alloys capable of dramatically reducing mech mass while maintaining extraordinary structural strength.

This allowed for: - larger frames - greater maneuverability - improved survivability - enhanced battlefield endurance

docs.title: Neural Interface Systems
docs.text:
The Imperium also developed advanced neural synchronization systems allowing pilots to control mechs through direct brain-machine interfacing.

These systems drastically improved: - reaction speed - precision - balance - movement coordination

For the first time, mechs became extensions of the pilot rather than simply vehicles.

docs.title: Compact Fusion Cores
docs.text:
Perhaps the most important breakthrough was the development of compact mobile fusion cores.

These miniature reactors generated unprecedented amounts of portable energy, allowing mechs to power: - advanced locomotion systems - heavy armor - energy weapons - sustained battlefield operations

without reliance on massive external infrastructure.

docs.title: The First True War Mechs
docs.text:
With these technologies combined, the first true war mechs emerged.

Although primitive by modern standards, these early machines represented a complete revolution in military capability.

Fortresses previously considered impregnable fell within days beneath the assault of the new mechanized giants.

Military doctrine throughout the Core changed almost overnight.

The sudden emergence of effective mech warfare catapulted the Imperium into an era of rapid expansion and military dominance.

Many historians regard this moment as the beginning of the modern age of warfare.

docs.title: The Spread of Mech Technology
docs.text:
Over time, the Imperium’s technological advantages gradually spread throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Whether through: - espionage - trade - defections - battlefield salvage - industrial imitation

the secrets of mech engineering eventually disseminated beyond Imperium control.

Mech technology rapidly became the military foundation of interstellar civilization itself.

As the centuries passed, each major faction developed its own: - mech doctrines - engineering traditions - combat philosophies - preferred weapon systems

Today, mech warfare defines nearly every major ground conflict throughout civilized space.

docs.title: The True Origins of the Mech?
docs.text:
Despite the Imperium’s historical association with mech development, some historians question whether the Lytherians truly invented the first war mech.

Critics argue there is limited evidence the early Lytherians possessed the scientific foundation necessary to independently create such revolutionary technology so rapidly.

Some theories suggest the Imperium may have: - discovered an earlier mechanized civilization - appropriated foreign technology - reverse-engineered recovered systems - inherited forgotten industrial knowledge

Others argue these theories are little more than historical revisionism aimed at undermining Imperium prestige.

No conclusive evidence has ever emerged.

The true origins of mech technology remain one of the great unresolved historical questions of the Core.

docs.title: Modern Mech Doctrine
docs.text: Although all major factions utilize mechs, each approaches mech warfare differently.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
• Confederate Vanguard Union Mechs

The Union emphasizes: - mass deployment - industrial production - coordinated mechanized offensives - sustained battlefield pressure

Union mech doctrine prioritizes overwhelming force and operational momentum.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
• Helios Sovereignty Mechs

The Sovereignty favors: - polished military formations - aristocratic officer traditions - prestige deployments - highly visible battlefield command

Sovereignty mech units are often viewed as symbols of status as much as military assets.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
• Omnisphere Imperium Mechs

The Imperium maintains some of the oldest continuous mech traditions in the Core.

Imperium doctrine emphasizes: - disciplined formations - elite pilots - battlefield endurance - strategic patience - heavy war steel

Fire symbolism remains deeply embedded within many Imperium mech traditions.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
• Starcrest Protectorate Mechs

Protectorate doctrine focuses heavily upon: - defensive warfare - fortified operations - territorial control - attritional combat

Protectorate mech units are widely respected for their durability and defensive coordination.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
• Orion Corporate Mechs

Orion mech doctrine emphasizes: - technological precision - advanced electronics - battlefield networking - tactical adaptability - engineering superiority

Orion forces frequently field some of the most sophisticated targeting and systems packages in the Core.

docs.title: Mercenary Mechs
docs.text:
Mercenary mech culture differs substantially from House military doctrine.

Unlike standardized House forces, mercenary mechs are often: - heavily modified - salvaged - rebuilt repeatedly - personalized - patched together from mixed components

Many mercenary pilots spend years maintaining the same mech frame through: - battlefield salvage - black-market parts - improvised repairs - Guild-certified rebuilds

As a result, mech identity often becomes deeply personal among mercenary pilots.

Some machines acquire names, histories, and reputations nearly as famous as their operators.

docs.title: Mech Pilots
docs.text:
Piloting a mech requires extensive neural synchronization training.

Because neural interfaces connect directly with pilot sensory systems, experienced mech pilots often describe their machines as extensions of their own bodies.

This creates an unusually intimate relationship between pilot and machine.

Among mech pilots, common slang includes: - punch out — emergency ejection - riding redline — pushing a fusion core beyond safe limits - cooked — reactor or heat overload - dead steel — irrecoverable mech destruction - ghost steel — abandoned or unidentified mechs

Veteran pilots are highly respected throughout much of the Core.

In some frontier regions, mech pilots are viewed almost as modern knights or warlords.

docs.title: Deployment and Logistics
docs.text:
Despite their battlefield effectiveness, mechs are extraordinarily expensive to deploy and maintain.

Factors affecting deployment include: - cargo capacity - dropship tonnage - reactor fuel - ammunition requirements - maintenance infrastructure - terrain compatibility

For mercenary companies especially, transport logistics often determine: - contract viability - operational flexibility - strategic reach - profitability

Many smaller companies select mech loadouts based as much upon transport economics as battlefield effectiveness.

Among Guild logistics officers, there is a common saying:

“Amateurs count kills. Professionals count cargo mass.”

docs.title: Cultural Importance
docs.text:
Over centuries, mechs have become deeply woven into the identity of the Core itself.

They appear throughout: - military traditions - political ceremonies - propaganda - entertainment - architecture - public memorials

Entire planetary economies revolve around: - mech manufacturing - salvage - reactor production - mech repair - pilot training

Throughout much of civilized space, mechs are viewed not merely as weapons —

but as symbols of humanity’s survival through centuries of war.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
Even after centuries of continuous development, mech technology continues evolving.

Modern advancements focus on: - reactor efficiency - neural synchronization - armor composites - targeting systems - mobility enhancement - electronic warfare integration

At the same time, ancient mech technology recovered from forgotten battlefields and lost archives continues to fuel speculation about what may have existed before recorded history.

Some historians quietly wonder whether modern civilization fully understands the machines upon which it has built its entire military order.

Others argue such questions no longer matter.

The age of the mech has already arrived —

and there is no world in the Core untouched by its shadow.

docs.title: Imperium Mechs
docs.text:
Imperium mech forces combine common Core platforms with fast, elegant, often serpentine machines built around mobility, opening volleys, and the doctrine of fierce beauty.

This roster includes both the common Core designs available to all major factions and the signature designs most closely associated with the Omnisphere Imperium.

docs.title: Roster Summary
docs.text: Mech Model Hornet HN-120 Lynx LNX-40E Outrider OR-640 Yeoman YM-10F Lineman LM-30C Lancer LNC-220 Longstrike LG-210 Champion CH-70K Bulldog BD-770 Marksman MK-350 Gladiator GL-882 Kingsguard KG-950 Zephyr ZPH-01 Drake DRK-06 Ronin RON-12 Pyrefox PYX-53 Incursor ICR-99 Jaguar JGR-72 Ocelot OCE-45 Umbrantarch UMB-39 Redeemer RDM-10 Nagara NGA-47 Drakkon DRK-82 Ordosmech ORD-07 Warclaw WCL-61 Nightwing NGT-W4 Onagor ONA-37 Praetorian PRA-97 Neomech NEO-60 Star Slayer SLY-10

docs.title: Hornet HN-120
docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Lynx LNX-40E
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Outrider OR-640
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Zephyr ZPH-01
docs.text: A relic of the Imperium’s dueling traditions, the Zephyr was designed for blinding speed, able to outpace heavier mechs with ease. Armed with a blaster and light lasers, it darts in, strikes, and vanishes, leaving foes bewildered. Its name speaks to its nature: swift as wind, impossible to grasp.

docs.title: Drake DRK-06
docs.text: The Drake was crafted in homage to the Imperium’s dragon symbology, its blaster and twin scorchers breathing fire across enemy lines. Lightly armored but terrifying up close, it thrives in ambushes and raids. Pilots often paint their machines with scaled patterns, reinforcing its image as a dragon reborn in steel.

docs.title: Ronin RON-12
docs.text: A mech for warriors without masters, the Ronin was adopted by Imperium mercenary bands during the Old Imperium. With rockets for sudden fury and a blaster for dueling, it embodies the lone fighter’s creed: self-reliance, defiance, and a readiness to sell its blade to any cause.

docs.title: Pyrefox PYX-53
docs.text: True to its name, the Pyrefox engulfs battlefields with blasters and fire weapons, making it a terror in close combat. Developed to cleanse rebellious enclaves, it carries both symbolic and practical weight—reminding enemies that resisting the Imperium will always end in flames.

docs.title: Incursor ICR-99
docs.text: A reckless raider’s machine, the Incursor is fast, lightly protected, and dangerously over-armed for its frame. Rockets, blasters, and lasers make it unpredictable and deadly in the hands of bold pilots. Many Imperium aces built their reputations in Incursors, treating each battle as both gamble and performance.

docs.title: Jaguar JGR-72
docs.text: Named for the fierce predator, the Jaguar embodies speed and ferocity. Its vulkan cannon and blaster combination allows it to tear through lighter foes, while its design prioritizes the swift, lethal strike over defense. In Imperium culture, Jaguars are seen as hunters born for war, and this mech carries that tradition.

docs.title: Ocelot OCE-45
docs.text: The Ocelot was built to haunt enemies at range, its heavy energy cannon and blasters striking before foes can retaliate. Though lightly armored, its eerie precision and sudden appearances earned it a reputation as a phantom of the battlefield. Among Imperium warriors, piloting a Ocelot is considered an honor tied to ancestral rites of death and remembrance.

docs.title: Umbrantarch UMB-39
docs.text: The Umbrantarch is a fast, fear-inducing strike mech armed with a heavy blaster and scorcher systems, optimized for repeated hit-and-run assaults. More than a combat platform, the Umbrantarch is regarded as a terror weapon—its silhouette emerging through fire and smoke like an omen. Umbrantarch pilots are said to walk with spirits, feared as much by allies as by enemies.

docs.title: Redeemer RDM-10
docs.text: The Redeemer is a fast, hard-hitting medium platform designed for decisive strikes against exposed targets. Its rockets and blasters allow it to deliver sudden, concentrated fire before repositioning. Redeemers are deployed where momentum must be seized quickly and held through force.

docs.title: Nagara NGA-47
docs.text: Built during an era of holy wars, the Nagara combined heavy firepower with symbolic weight. With its HEC and heavy Vulkan cannon, it was designed to break enemy champions and restore order. To this day, the Imperium frames Nagaras as machines of salvation through fire and steel.

docs.title: Drakkon DRK-82
docs.text: A monstrous war machine, the Drakkon is drenched in flamethrowers and blasters, spewing destruction in every direction. It earned its name not from myth, but from its battlefield presence—unyielding, terrifying, and impossible to ignore. To the Imperium, the Drakkon is both dragon and executioner.

docs.title: Ordosmech ORD-07
docs.text: The Ordosmech embodies the Imperium’s martial faith, built as a crusader’s machine. Armed with Vulkan cannons, rockets, and blasters, it balances mass firepower with religious symbolism. Pilots clad their Ordosmechs in heraldry and ritual markings, treating each battle as both ritual war and ancestral duty.

docs.title: Warclaw WCL-61
docs.text: The Warclaw was commissioned during the Imperium’s early expansion campaigns, when rapid assaults proved insufficient against fortified resistance. Designed for prolonged engagements, it combines blasters, Vulkan cannons, and scorcher systems to apply constant pressure while advancing methodically. The Warclaw is deployed when territory must be taken and held, not merely breached.

docs.title: Nightwing NGT-W4
docs.text: The Nightwing prowls battlefields like a shadow, unleashing rockets, flamethrowers, and blaster fire before fading back into darkness. Heavily armed for its size, it embodies the Imperium’s preference for intimidation as well as annihilation. Its name, like its silhouette, evokes wings spread across the night sky.

docs.title: Onagor ONA-37
docs.text: The Onagor was built as a shock unit, carrying heavy vulkans and blasters to overwhelm enemies with sheer volume of fire. Though slower than some peers, it compensates with raw, thunderous destruction. Onagor is an ancient Imperium name, drawn from pre-unification myths describing a colossal entity whose advance was said to end wars by erasing the ground they were fought on.

docs.title: Praetorian PRA-97
docs.text: The Praetorian is one of the Imperium’s most feared and recognizable war machines. With paired heavy energy cannons, blasters, and scorchers, it combines versatile firepower with an imposing silhouette that dominates the field. More than a frontline brawler, the Praetorian is a commander’s mech, deployed to anchor formations and inspire dread in equal measure.

docs.title: Neomech NEO-60
docs.text: Born of experiments in massed rocket fire, the Neomech is a heavy line unit that drowns enemies in salvos while its blasters and lasers cut down survivors. Its name reflects both its modernity and its connection to the Imperium’s constant cycle of rebirth: old forms reforged into new engines of war.

docs.title: Star Slayer SLY-10
docs.text: The Star Slayer represents the Imperium’s mythic ambition. With heavy energy cannons and blasters, it was designed as a “god-killer,” capable of standing against the mightiest machines of rival Houses. Its name is both boast and prophecy—declaring that no star, no throne, and no rival shines brighter than the Imperium.

docs.title: Orion Mechs
docs.text:
Orion mech forces combine standard Core platforms with technologically advanced machines emphasizing sensors, precision, experimental systems, and battlefield efficiency.

This roster includes both the common Core designs available to all major factions and the signature designs most closely associated with the Orion Corporate.

docs.text: Mech Model Hornet HN-120 Lynx LNX-40E Outrider OR-640 Yeoman YM-10F Lineman LM-30C Lancer LNC-220 Longstrike LG-210 Champion CH-70K Bulldog BD-770 Marksman MK-350 Gladiator GL-882 Kingsguard KG-950 Photon PHN-50T Tachyon TCY-2R Zeno ZEN-400P Darius DR-300S Cobalt CLT-B1 Watcher WAT-002 Deviance DEV-2N Xerxes XER-200A Echelon ECH-4R Blaze Bringer BLZ-360K Reckoner REK-4M Eradicator ERAD-120R Zealot ZEL-20T Endermech END-550M Markov MKV-60L Rift Master RFM-220C Nebuchadnezzar NEB-Z001 Aeon AE-550X

Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Outrider OR-640
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Photon PHN-50T
docs.text: Developed by the Sentinel Program under the Orion Standards Commission, the Photon was the first widely fielded scout with projected shielding. Its medium prismatic array doubles as a sensor-liaison tool, letting it tag targets and survive brushes that would scrap other lights.

docs.title: Tachyon TCY-2R
docs.text: An offshoot of the Photon II trials, the Tachyon prioritized velocity and precision fire for counter-raider screens. Twin light and one medium prismatic give it sharp, repeatable bursts, while its avionics tie directly into early-warning buoys. On border nets, “tachyon alert” means a fast mover has already cut the angle.

docs.title: Zeno ZEN-400P
docs.text: Created for medium-range interdiction, the Zeno pairs a medium prismatic with economical rocket salvos to wear down fleeing targets. Modular hardpoints and easy field service made it a favorite of Citizen Guard patrols. Its name nods to pursuit paradoxes—once a Zeno starts the chase, the gap never seems to close for the prey.

docs.title: Darius DR-300S
docs.text: Commissioned by the Heavy Systems Bureau to give citizen brigades a dependable lineholder, the Darius blends twin medium prismatics with stabilizing shields and guided missiles. It lacks flourish but excels at steady positional warfare, advancing behind a rational wall of data and fire. Crews respect it for what it is: the dependable center of an orderly advance.

docs.title: Cobalt CLT-B1
docs.text: A precision striker from the Alloy Labs Initiative, the Cobalt mates a heavy energy cannon to a medium prismatic under reinforced shields. Its distinctive blue ceramic laminate isn’t for show—it dissipates heat and mitigates spall. Graduation units from several academies award “Cobalt keys” to pilots certified on its demanding fire-control regimen.

docs.title: Watcher WAT-002
docs.text: Designed as a mesh-linked sentry, the Watcher stitches missiles, rockets, and prismatics into the battlespace network, shaping enemy movement rather than chasing it. When a Watcher is present, commanders talk about lanes and closures—the field becomes a diagram, and the diagram favors Orion.

docs.title: Deviance DEV-2N
docs.text: The Deviance began as an unsanctioned field refit, where pilots overloaded a standard chassis with heavy prismatic arrays and rockets beyond recommended tolerances. Though the Assembly initially condemned the design, its brutal effectiveness in trials forced acceptance. Its name stuck, a reminder that sometimes progress comes from breaking rules.

docs.title: Xerxes XER-200A
docs.text: Part of the Archives’ “Kings Series” naming convention, the Xerxes is an expeditionary spearhead with twin heavy prismatics, rockets, and resilient shields. It isn’t subtle—its job is to impose order at the point of contact. Citizen brigades treat it like a mobile verdict: the argument ends where Xerxes stands.

docs.title: Echelon ECH-4R
docs.text: Built for charter enforcement and clean disables, the Echelon uses paired light prismatics and mixed missiles to peel weapons without catastrophic kills. Its telemetry suite prioritizes limb and mount solutions over cores. Echelons are routinely assigned to interdiction details, where compliance is required intact rather than destroyed.

docs.title: Blaze Bringer BLZ-360K
docs.text: A field-illumination and suppression platform, the Blaze Bringer throws a curtain of coherent light—medium and light prismatics that dazzle sensors and scour cover. High sink capacity lets it sustain the show long after others cool off. Night operations doctrine calls it “turning darkness to glass.”

docs.title: Reckoner REK-4M
docs.text: The Reckoner serves as an anchor and command relay. Heavy and medium prismatics handle threat nodes while a rocket bank shapes approaches. Its hardened comms and fire-control links allow it to coordinate allied units under sustained contact. Once established, a Reckoner position is intended to be held, not redeployed.

docs.title: Eradicator ERAD-120R
docs.text: Despite the severe name, the Eradicator is a surgical siege tool. A heavy energy cannon and twin prismatics cut precise breach patterns while rockets clear debris and counter-pushes. City engineers like working behind it; the hole it makes is the hole the plan expected.

docs.title: Zealot ZEL-20T
docs.text: Adopted enthusiastically by Citizen Guard auxiliaries, the Zealot layers shields and a full prismatic suite to hold ground with conviction. Rockets punish flanking attempts while heavy and medium beams keep pressure steady. Its “zeal” isn’t mysticism—just the civic habit of not giving an inch.

docs.title: Endermech END-550M
docs.text: The Endermech serves as a versatile line-breaker, protected by advanced shielding. Armed with a heavy energy cannon, twin heavy prismatics, and missile support, it can adapt to almost any battlefield role. Pilots often remark on its uncanny precision, as if the machine anticipates threats a moment before they emerge.

docs.title: Markov MKV-60L
docs.text: The Markov integrates predictive targeting from the Markov Kernel Project with twin heavy prismatics and deep heat reserves. Pilots joke it only needs to lock once; after that the future is already computed. In analytics drills, its variance charts are a thing of austere beauty.

docs.title: Rift Master RFM-220C
docs.text: The Rift Master bends battlefields to its will. A chokepoint dominator, it seals narrow passes with heavy prismatic fire and rocket storms. Its shield lattice is tuned for glancing shots from extreme angles—perfect for canyon walls and megastructure corridors.

docs.title: Nebuchadnezzar NEB-Z001
docs.text: A prestige fortress platform from the Archives’ “City-Builder” lineage, the Nebuchadnezzar layers heavy, medium, and light prismatics with compliant missiles under deep shields. It’s meant to hold capitals and corridors, projecting calm inevitability. The name fits: it builds order by unbuilding resistance.

docs.title: Aeon AE-550X
docs.text: The Aeon is the pinnacle of Corporate technology, a mech designed to embody timeless supremacy. Quad heavy prismatics gave it unrivaled firepower, while advanced shields can make it feel invincible. Corporate propaganda has declared it ‘the machine that will outlast eternity.’

docs.title: Protectorate Mechs
docs.text:
Protectorate mech forces combine shared Core workhorses with rugged heavy machines designed for endurance, harsh-environment combat, and holding defensive lines under extreme pressure.

This roster includes both the common Core designs available to all major factions and the signature designs most closely associated with the Starcrest Protectorate.

docs.title: Roster Summary
docs.text: Mech Model Hornet HN-120 Lynx LNX-40E Outrider OR-640 Yeoman YM-10F Lineman LM-30C Lancer LNC-220 Longstrike LG-210 Champion CH-70K Bulldog BD-770 Marksman MK-350 Gladiator GL-882 Kingsguard KG-950 War Dog WAR-D1 Reaver RVR-N5 Jackal JCK-L4 Harbinger HAR-G9 Beast BST-R3 Instigator INS-G9 Razorback RZK-7B Neanderthal NEA-R8 Barbarian BAR-N8 Overlord OVR-L1 Berserker BER-Z3 Thunder God TDR-G7 Infernal INF-L9 Doomhammer DOOM-H2 Brute Machine BRT-100R Cromagnon CRM-N2 Juggernaut JGR-N6 Ragnarok RGK-5000K

docs.title: Hornet HN-120
docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Lynx LNX-40E
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Outrider OR-640
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: War Dog WAR-D1
docs.text: One of the oldest fielded designs in the Protectorate arsenal, the War Dog earned its name in decades of border wars where packs of them ran down raiders under cover of smoke. Reliable and loyal in function, it remains a cornerstone of Protectorate doctrine.

docs.title: Reaver RVR-N5
