docs.text: First deployed during the Black Hills Campaign, the Reaver became infamous for tearing through lighter mechs with its crushing fist and rockets. Its brutal reputation lingers; to Protectorate troops, a Reaver on the field is a promise of broken lines.

docs.title: Jackal JCK-L4
docs.text: Known as the “stormcrow” of the Protectorate, the Harbinger often appears at the outset of offensives, signaling devastation to come. Its combination of fists, vulkan, and lasers makes it a herald of annihilation as much as a front-line bruiser.

docs.title: Harbinger HAR-G9
docs.text: Named for its role as a herald of destruction, the Harbinger combines brute force with relentless suppressive fire. Its battle fist and vulkan cannon hammer enemies at close range, while laser support ensures there is no safe angle of approach.

docs.title: Beast BST-R3
docs.text: The Beast traces back to Protectorate shock-divisions raised from penal battalions. Pilots dubbed it for its savage ultra-vulkan recoil and feral silhouette, and the name stuck. Today it remains a terror weapon, more feared than refined.

docs.title: Instigator INS-G9
docs.text: The Instigator is a line-breaker, built to provoke chaos. Its heavy cannon and rocket payload drive enemies from cover, while laser fire harasses stragglers. True to its name, it starts fights the Protectorate intends to finish.

docs.title: Razorback RZK-7B
docs.text: With its distinctive armored ridges, the Razorback resembles the boar for which it is named. It first entered service during the Dust Marches, where its heavy autogun and laser battery carved corridors through smoke-choked canyons.

docs.title: Neanderthal NEA-R8
docs.text: The Neanderthal earned its name during testing, where its sheer, blunt brutality demolished mock fortresses in minutes. It remains the Protectorate’s premier siege breaker, a mech that embodies unrefined but devastating power.

docs.title: Barbarian BAR-N8
docs.text: The Barbarian embodies the Protectorate’s doctrine of raw shock power. With its crushing fist, heavy lasers, and layered autoguns, it thrives in brutal close-quarters fighting where finesse is meaningless and only overwhelming force decides the outcome.

docs.title: Overlord OVR-L1
docs.text: The Overlord was created as a command mech for Protectorate strike groups, its size and armament meant to dominate attention. Though later fielded in greater numbers, its reputation as a battlefield overseer still clings to it.

docs.title: Berserker BER-Z3
docs.text: The Berserker channels raw ferocity into every engagement. Its crushing fist and banks of medium lasers let it drive headlong into enemy lines, while smoke rounds shroud its reckless advance. It is feared less for precision than for the chaos it unleashes up close.

docs.title: Thunder God TDR-G7
docs.text: The Thunder God takes its name from its battlefield presence—heavy cannons and vulkans that echo like storms breaking over the horizon. Protectorate lore holds that its arrival signals the final hammerblow of a campaign.

docs.title: Infernal INF-L9
docs.text: The Infernal’s earliest deployments left entire valleys scorched black, and its fire-spewing design cemented its apocalyptic reputation. Protectorate commanders often use it as a psychological weapon as much as a physical one.

docs.title: Doomhammer DOOM-H2
docs.text: The Doomhammer was first forged to break stone bunkers and hardened enemy positions. With massive vulkans and crushing fists, it still fulfills that grim role, delivering hammerblows of smoke and fire to erase dug-in resistance.

docs.title: Brute Machine BRT-100R
docs.text: A product of rushed wartime design, the Brute Machine was derided for its inelegance but proved itself by sheer weight of fire. Its survival and adoption are proof of the Protectorate’s core belief: overwhelming force wins wars.

docs.title: Cromagnon CRM-N2
docs.text: The Cromagnon’s name came from rival houses mocking its primitive simplicity—until Protectorate pilots smashed through their most advanced fortifications. Now it is worn with pride, a blunt instrument that proves sophistication isn’t always required.

docs.title: Juggernaut JGR-N6
docs.text: The second heaviest mech ever fielded in the Core, the Juggernaut was first deployed to break the sieges of the Border Wars, where its unstoppable advance became legend. With layered armor, a crushing fist, and a mix of rockets, lasers, and autoguns, it grinds forward until resistance collapses. Entire fortresses have fallen simply by being in its path.

docs.title: Ragnarok RGK-5000K
docs.text: The largest mech ever fielded, the Ragnarok is the end made metal. A terrifying opponent in close ranges, it is heavily armed with vulkans, cannons, and scorcher systems and turns battlefields into apocalyptic wastelands. Wherever it marches, smoke and ruin follow, sealing the fate of all who oppose the Protectorate.

docs.title: Mercenaries
docs.text: “No war is won for free.” — Mercenary saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Common Name Metal Mercs Primary Hub Drake’s Landing Primary Authority Mercenary Guild Primary Equipment Combat Mechs Primary Occupation Private Military Contracting Common Saying “No House Rules The Frontier.”

Mercenaries — commonly referred to throughout civilized space as Metal Mercs — are private military companies operating throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Although officially independent, mercenary organizations play a central role in the political and military balance of the modern era.

Mercenary companies are employed for: - security operations - frontier defense - anti-piracy campaigns - industrial protection - proxy conflicts - recon contracts - convoy escort - quiet work - private warfare

While the Great Houses officially prohibit open warfare against one another under the authority of the Star Regency, mercenary companies provide a means through which conflicts can occur without escalating into civilization-wide war.

As a result, mercenaries have become one of the defining institutions of modern Core society.

Throughout the frontier, many civilians view mercenary companies as both protectors and predators — armed outsiders arriving under Guild seal, contract slate in hand, and reactor smoke on the horizon.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
Mercenary warfare expanded rapidly during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

As civilization stabilized, tensions between the Great Houses increasingly shifted away from direct military confrontation and toward: - covert operations - frontier disputes - proxy conflicts - deniable warfare

Independent military companies proved ideal for these operations.

Mercenaries allowed the Houses to: - project force - secure frontier systems - influence unstable regions - pursue political objectives

without openly violating Conclave law.

Over time, mercenary culture evolved into a permanent and highly influential part of interstellar civilization.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
Most recognized mercenary companies operate under the authority of the Mercenary Guild.

The Guild functions as a semi-neutral regulatory authority responsible for: - contract arbitration - licensing - company registration - dispute mediation - reputation tracking - blacklisting - payment enforcement

The Guild officially operates independently from the Great Houses, though nearly every major power attempts to influence Guild operations to some extent.

Without Guild recognition, a company may struggle to secure legitimate contracts through the Open Board or gain access to reliable salvage markets within civilized space.

The Guild’s central headquarters are located on Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: Drake’s Landing
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing is widely regarded as the mercenary capital of the Core.

The world functions as: - a hiring center - contract exchange hub - mech repair world - training center - recruitment station - black market trade hub

Thousands of independent pilots, technicians, officers, and mercenary companies pass through Drake’s Landing every year.

The world possesses a reputation for: - opportunity - violence - corruption - ambition - independence

Throughout the frontier, many aspiring mech pilots dream of one day building their own company on Landing and earning a verified steel registration through the Guild.

docs.title: Mercenary Companies
docs.text:
Mercenary companies vary enormously in size and capability.

Some consist of only: - a handful of pilots - a transport vessel - a few aging mechs

Others rival the standing militaries of smaller planetary governments.

Many companies specialize in: - heavy assault - recon operations - frontier survival - anti-piracy warfare - urban combat - convoy protection - industrial security

A company’s reputation is often more valuable than its military strength.

Successful companies survive through: - reliability - professionalism - battlefield performance - loyalty - strategic reputation

Poor reputations can rapidly destroy a company’s access to contracts, financing, and Open Board opportunities.

docs.title: Free Jacks
docs.text:
Not all mercenaries belong to large companies or long-standing mercenary commands.

Independent operators known as Free Jacks work outside permanent affiliations, taking contracts wherever work can be found.

Some Free Jacks operate alone in single mechs.

Others form temporary crews or short-term charter groups assembled for specific contracts.

Free Jacks are common throughout: - frontier systems - salvage zones - pirate regions - unstable trade routes

Many eventually join larger companies after building enough reputation on the Open Board.

Others remain fiercely independent throughout their careers.

Within mercenary culture, Free Jacks possess a mixed reputation.

Some are respected as adaptable frontier veterans capable of surviving without House sponsorship or major company support.

Others are viewed as unreliable drifters, reactor gamblers, or walking debt barely holding their steel together long enough to survive another contract cycle.

Despite this reputation, many legendary mercenary commanders began their careers as Free Jacks operating from Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: Mechs and Mercenary Life
docs.text:
Mechs are central to mercenary culture.

Owning and maintaining combat mechs is enormously expensive, making mech ownership both prestigious and financially dangerous.

Many mercenary pilots are: - former military personnel - frontier survivors - displaced nobles - failed officers - salvagers - opportunists - ex-House pilots

A successful company may spend years accumulating: - salvage - replacement parts - weapons - reactors - transport ships - experienced crew

The destruction of even a single mech can financially cripple smaller companies.

Many independent pilots spend most of their careers riding redline financially, one bad contract or failed salvage claim away from getting dry docked permanently.

As a result, mercenary commanders often become highly pragmatic regarding: - risk - contract terms - salvage rights - reactor strain - battlefield engagement

Among veteran mercs, there is a common saying:

“A smart pilot knows when to punch out. A dead pilot keeps fighting.”

docs.title: Reputation and Morality
docs.text:
Mercenary companies possess wildly different reputations throughout the Core.

Some companies are viewed as: - honorable professionals - defenders of frontier settlements - reliable military contractors

Others are little more than heavily armed criminals operating under legal cover.

The line between: - mercenary - pirate - warlord - private army

can become dangerously thin in unstable frontier regions.

The Mercenary Guild officially condemns piracy and civilian attacks, though enforcement remains inconsistent outside major trade routes.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses employ mercenary forces to varying degrees.

Officially, mercenary contracts are used primarily for: - frontier security - anti-piracy operations - infrastructure defense

Unofficially, many companies participate in: - proxy wars - political sabotage - territorial disputes - covert military operations - quiet work

House sponsorship of mercenary companies is common, though often concealed through intermediaries and shell contracts.

The Star Regency officially discourages excessive mercenary escalation, but most political observers recognize that mercenary warfare has become one of the primary mechanisms preventing open conflict between the Great Houses themselves.

As one Guild analyst famously summarized:

“The Houses call it peace because somebody else signs the contracts.”

docs.title: Frontier Importance
docs.text:
Beyond the Core, mercenary companies often represent the closest thing frontier systems possess to organized military protection.

Many isolated settlements rely upon mercenary forces for: - pirate defense - convoy escort - local security - emergency response - law enforcement

In some regions, powerful mercenary companies effectively function as independent governments.

This has made mercenary culture deeply intertwined with frontier identity.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, mercenaries are viewed with a mixture of: - admiration - suspicion - fear - romanticism - distrust

To some, mercenaries represent freedom, independence, and opportunity beyond the rigid structures of the Great Houses.

To others, they are profiteers who prolong instability for personal gain.

Despite these criticisms, few deny the enormous influence mercenary companies now wield throughout the Core and frontier alike.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As political tensions rise throughout the Core and instability spreads across frontier space, demand for mercenary services continues to increase.

Many analysts within StarCom and the Regency fear that the growing dependence upon private military forces reflects a deeper weakening of interstellar political stability.

Others argue mercenaries have become an essential pressure valve preventing civilization from descending into open inter-house war.

Whatever the truth, one fact remains clear:

the modern age of the Core belongs as much to the Metal Mercs as it does to the Great Houses themselves.

docs.title: Greater Mercenary Companies
docs.text: “The Houses rule worlds. The Greater Companies decide who survives between them.” — Guild strategic assessment

docs.title: :material-fort: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Large Independent Mercenary Commands Operational Scale Multi-System Economic Role Private Military Powers Territorial Reach Several Worlds Primary Oversight Mercenary Guild Common Reputation “States Without Thrones”

Greater Mercenary Companies are the largest and most powerful mercenary organizations operating throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Unlike ordinary mercenary units, Greater Companies function as massive institutional military organizations possessing: - fleets - logistics networks - recruitment infrastructure - repair facilities - long-term charters - standing military formations

Some rival smaller planetary governments in military capability and economic influence.

Although technically private military organizations, Greater Companies often possess enough political leverage to influence: - frontier stability - trade security - regional conflicts - House relations - Guild policy

Throughout civilized space, they are viewed simultaneously as: - military contractors - strategic assets - political actors - stabilizing forces - potential threats

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The rise of the Greater Companies began during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

As mercenary warfare became increasingly normalized throughout the Core, certain successful companies accumulated enough: - wealth - salvage - territory - personnel - logistical capability

to expand far beyond the scale of traditional contract forces.

Over generations, some evolved from relatively small mercenary outfits into massive military institutions operating across multiple systems.

Unlike temporary frontier companies or independent Free Jack crews, Greater Companies developed permanent organizational structures capable of surviving beyond the lifespan of individual commanders.

This transformed them from simple mercenary units into enduring military powers.

docs.title: The Three Greater Companies
docs.text:
Although countless mercenary companies operate throughout civilized space, only three are universally recognized as the true Greater Mercenary Companies of the modern era.

These organizations possess such immense military and logistical capability that many political analysts consider them quasi-state powers operating outside direct House authority.

docs.title: The Nomad Company
docs.text:
Supreme Commander Field Lord Combat Doctrine Relentless Assault Warfare Primary Asset Mobile Dreadnought Fortress Frequent Employer Starcrest Protectorate

The Nomad Company is one of the largest and most feared mercenary organizations in the Core.

The company is renowned for deploying overwhelming battlefield force through: - massive mech formations - sustained assaults - aggressive maneuver warfare - attritional campaigns

The Nomads possess a reputation for capturing enemy equipment intact whenever possible, allowing them to expand and maintain their forces through battlefield salvage as much as formal production.

Unlike many large mercenary commands, the Nomad Company maintains no permanent planetary capital.

Instead, its operational headquarters is housed within a heavily modified dreadnought battle cruiser capable of sustaining long-term deep-space operations.

This mobile fortress serves simultaneously as: - command center - fleet anchor - training base - logistics hub - symbolic homeworld

The company officially accepts contracts from all Great Houses, though it maintains especially close operational ties with the Starcrest Protectorate.

Among mercenaries, Nomad forces are often viewed as relentless, disciplined, and extraordinarily difficult to destroy completely.

docs.title: The Warforged Convoy
docs.text:
Supreme Commander War Marshal Combat Doctrine Adaptive Mechanized Warfare Specialization Heavy Mech Modification Frequent Employer Confederate Vanguard Union

The Warforged Convoy is famous throughout the Core for its heavily modified and extensively customized mech forces.

The company’s mechs are maintained and redesigned by highly skilled mechwrights capable of radically altering battlefield configurations between deployments.

Warforged pilots are often trained as both: - mech operators - field engineers - combat technicians

This allows Warforged units to rapidly adapt equipment and weapon systems to changing battlefield conditions.

Enemy commanders frequently struggle to identify Warforged units accurately, as the company’s modified mechs rarely conform to standard military specifications.

The Convoy’s flexible combat doctrine and engineering culture have earned it a formidable reputation across frontier warzones.

The Confederate Vanguard Union is known to employ the Warforged Convoy extensively for: - offensive campaigns - mechanized breakthroughs - industrial warfare - heavy assault operations

Within mercenary circles, the Warforged are respected for their technical expertise and feared for the unpredictability of their steel.

docs.title: Iron Phoenix Regulars
docs.text:
Supreme Commander Red Commander Combat Doctrine Strategic Precision Operations Specialization Elite Raids and Tactical Operations Frequent Employers Helios Sovereignty and Omnisphere Imperium

The Iron Phoenix Regulars are widely regarded as among the most disciplined and strategically sophisticated mercenary forces in civilized space.

Unlike the overwhelming assaults favored by the Nomad Company or the adaptive mechanized warfare of the Warforged Convoy, the Regulars specialize in: - precise operational planning - elite strike missions - strategic raids - extraction operations - targeted battlefield disruption

The Regulars maintain an unusually strict internal code of conduct by mercenary standards.

This professionalism has earned them considerable respect throughout the Core, including among many rival commanders.

Their elite strike teams are particularly famous for conducting high-profile operations capable of influencing broader geopolitical events.

The Helios Sovereignty and Omnisphere Imperium frequently employ the Iron Phoenix Regulars for missions requiring: - discretion - coordination - strategic discipline - political sensitivity

Among mercenary circles, the Regulars are often viewed as the closest thing the profession possesses to a true elite officer corps.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses employ Greater Mercenary Companies to varying degrees.

These organizations are especially valuable for: - frontier stabilization - anti-piracy campaigns - strategic deniability - expeditionary warfare - border conflicts - politically sensitive operations

Although technically independent, many Greater Companies maintain long-standing historical ties with specific Houses.

Some are widely believed to function as unofficial House auxiliaries despite maintaining nominal neutrality.

The Houses tolerate the existence of Greater Companies because they provide military flexibility without requiring direct inter-house warfare.

At the same time, many political leaders remain deeply uncomfortable with the sheer military power these organizations possess.

docs.title: Relationship with the Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild plays a critical role in regulating Greater Companies.

Because of their scale and strategic importance, Greater Companies are deeply integrated into: - Guild arbitration systems - charter regulation - contract enforcement - salvage oversight - reputation systems

Many Greater Companies possess substantial political influence within Guild institutions themselves.

Smaller mercenary outfits occasionally accuse the Guild of favoring Greater Companies through: - preferential contracts - arbitration bias - salvage rulings - enforcement discretion

Guild officials strongly deny such accusations.

docs.title: Lesser and Minor Companies
docs.text:
Below the Greater Companies exist countless: - Lesser Mercenary Companies - Minor Mercenary Companies - Free Jack crews

The overwhelming majority of mercenary organizations throughout the Core fall into these smaller classifications.

Many survive by accepting: - subcontract work - escort charters - salvage operations - local defense contracts - anti-piracy assignments

from Greater Companies.

This creates a layered mercenary economy in which major military operations frequently involve networks of subordinate or affiliated companies operating beneath larger banners.

docs.title: Mercenary Culture
docs.text:
Greater Companies possess powerful internal cultures and identities.

Veteran mercenaries often identify more strongly with their company banner than with any Great House.

Large companies develop: - traditions - heraldry - rivalries - operational doctrines - command philosophies - internal myths

Among mercenaries, reputation matters enormously.

A respected company banner may attract: - elite pilots - favorable contracts - long charters - House sponsorship - premium Guild access

while a burned banner may struggle to survive.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, the Greater Mercenary Companies are viewed with a mixture of: - admiration - fear - dependence - resentment - respect

To frontier worlds, they may represent the only reliable military protection available.

To the Great Houses, they are useful but potentially dangerous strategic actors.

To independent mercenaries, they are often viewed either as: - aspirational legends - bloated institutions - contract machines - or the ultimate destination of a successful mercenary career

As one old Landing saying goes:

“Minor crews chase contracts. Greater Companies chase sectors.”

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability spreads throughout the Core and frontier alike, the influence of the Greater Mercenary Companies continues to grow.

Some StarCom analysts privately warn that several Greater Companies now possess enough military and logistical capability to destabilize entire sectors should they ever fully abandon Guild oversight.

Others argue the Greater Companies have become indispensable to maintaining modern civilization itself.

For now, the balance holds.

The Great Houses still rule the Core.

But increasingly, the wars between them are fought not beneath House banners —

but beneath the steel banners of the Greater Companies.

docs.title: Lesser Mercenary Companies
docs.text: “Greater Companies shape sectors. Lesser Companies win wars.” — Mercenary Guild tactical saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Mid-to-Large Mercenary Commands Operational Scale Regional and Multi-System Military Capability Multiple Divisions Primary Strength Specialized Warfare Doctrine Guild Classification Lesser Mercenary Company Common Reputation “Professional Steel”

In the dynamic and often volatile mercenary landscape of the Core, Lesser Mercenary Companies hold a crucial position.

While they may not possess the overwhelming force and extensive resources of the Greater Mercenary Companies, these units are nonetheless formidable military organizations in their own right.

Most Lesser Companies command: - multiple divisions - dedicated logistics staff - permanent bases - transport fleets - mech repair infrastructure - private starships

Their scale allows them to operate effectively across frontier regions without requiring the enormous economic and logistical structures associated with the Greater Companies.

Unlike many smaller mercenary units, Lesser Companies frequently specialize in highly refined combat doctrines, making them valuable strategic assets for the Great Houses, frontier governments, and Guild-chartered operations.

Though Lesser Mercenary Companies are sufficiently large to have often worked alongside multiple Great Houses throughout their histories, many develop long-standing preferences, loyalties, or operational relationships with particular factions.

docs.title: Role in the Core
docs.text:
Lesser Companies occupy a critical middle ground within mercenary civilization.

They are: - larger and more stable than independent companies - more flexible than the Greater Companies - capable of long-term campaigns - specialized enough for precision operations - small enough to deploy rapidly

Because of this, Lesser Companies are frequently hired for: - regional conflicts - planetary campaigns - strategic raids - siege operations - defensive warfare - urban pacification - deep frontier deployments

Guild analysts often describe Lesser Companies as the backbone of modern mercenary warfare.

docs.title: Military Identity
docs.text:
Unlike the Greater Companies, which frequently function as enormous military institutions, Lesser Companies tend to build reputations around: - battlefield doctrine - tactical specialization - command philosophy - elite operational capability

Their banners are often associated with highly recognizable forms of warfare.

Experienced commanders throughout the Core frequently select Lesser Companies based upon: - terrain - operational style - political sensitivity - mission profile - required strategic flexibility

Among mercenaries, a respected Lesser Company banner carries enormous prestige.

docs.title: Wardens of the Astral Empire
docs.text:
Specialization Defensive Warfare and Siege Defense Preferred Equipment Heavy Assault Mechs Battlefield Role Last-Line Defensive Operations

The Wardens of the Astral Empire are among the most respected defensive specialists in the Core.

Frequently contracted to hold critical defensive positions during: - sieges - planetary invasions - attritional campaigns - large-scale battles

the Wardens are renowned for their ability to endure under overwhelming pressure.

Their formations emphasize: - layered defenses - fortified positions - overlapping fire zones - disciplined heavy mech deployment

The Wardens are widely regarded as one of the few mercenary forces capable of stabilizing collapsing battlefronts after other units have failed.

docs.title: Starfall Exarchs
docs.text:
Specialization Guerrilla Warfare and Deep Recon Commander Commander Lyra Skye Signature Tactic Sky Strike

Led by Commander Lyra Skye, the Starfall Exarchs specialize in: - long-range reconnaissance - deep frontier warfare - surprise assaults - hostile territory operations - prolonged unsupported campaigns

Their pilots are rigorously trained to survive under extreme operational conditions with limited logistical support.

The Exarchs are particularly famous for their Sky Strike doctrine, involving high-altitude combat drops directly into enemy strongholds.

These sudden assaults often disrupt command structures before defenders can properly organize resistance.

Throughout the Core, the Starfall Exarchs are respected for their independence, adaptability, and operational precision.

docs.title: Havoc’s Sandstorm Legion
docs.text:
Specialization Rapid Assault Warfare Combat Style Mobility and Disruption :material-desert: Preferred Terrain Desert and Wasteland Operations

Havoc’s Sandstorm Legion thrives in chaotic battlefield environments.

The Legion specializes in: - rapid mechanized assaults - battlefield disruption - fast-strike operations - mobility warfare - aggressive flanking tactics

Their mech forces emphasize speed and maneuverability, often darting through enemy formations to strike vulnerable assets before disappearing into dust, smoke, and debris.

The Legion is particularly effective in: - desert warfare - wasteland conflicts - low-visibility environments - unstable frontier terrain

Enemy commanders frequently describe Sandstorm assaults as disorienting and psychologically exhausting.

docs.title: Gravewalker’s Horde
docs.text:
:material-cannon: Specialization Heavy Assault and Shock Warfare Founder The Gravewalker Primary Role Fortification Breach Operations

Gravewalker’s Horde is infamous throughout the Core for its relentless offensive doctrine.

The company combines: - heavy artillery - shock troops - armored breakthroughs - concentrated assault formations

to overwhelm even heavily fortified positions.

The company was founded by the legendary mercenary commander known only as The Gravewalker, a figure reputed to have never lost a battle.

Stories surrounding the Gravewalker have become deeply mythologized throughout mercenary culture.

Many accounts describe him personally leading assaults from the front lines during seemingly hopeless battles before turning certain defeat into decisive victory.

Whether all such stories are true remains widely debated.

docs.title: The Shadowland Drifters
docs.text:
Specialization Tactical Deception and Ambush Warfare Combat Style Indirect Engagement Operational Role Disruption Operations

The Shadowland Drifters are masters of stealth, tactical deception, and battlefield disruption.

Rather than engaging in direct confrontation, the Drifters specialize in: - ambush tactics - infiltration - misdirection - indirect engagement - strategic harassment

Their operations frequently focus on weakening enemy formations and creating opportunities for larger allied offensives.

The Drifters are especially effective at dismantling: - supply lines - sensor grids - communications infrastructure - defensive coordination

before enemies fully realize they are under attack.

Their interventions have altered the outcomes of numerous major campaigns despite involving relatively limited direct combat.

docs.title: Abyssal Marauders
docs.text:
Specialization High-Risk Operations Commander Major Jorah Helm :material-rescue: Operational Role Rescue and Asset Recovery

Led by the infamous Major Jorah Helm, the Abyssal Marauders are widely regarded as the daredevils of the mercenary world.

The Marauders routinely accept: - impossible contracts - deep extraction missions - hazardous rescue operations - asset recovery campaigns - high-risk frontier deployments

that many other companies refuse outright.

Their missions frequently involve recovering personnel, technology, or strategic resources capable of altering regional balances of power.

The Marauders possess a reputation for recklessness, but also for succeeding under conditions where conventional military planning has already failed.

docs.title: Ark’s Outcast Guardians
docs.text:
Specialization Urban Warfare and Pacification Combat Style Rapid Deployment Primary Role Settlement and Base Security

Ark’s Outcast Guardians are among the most reliable urban warfare specialists operating within the Core.

The company excels at: - close-quarters combat - rapid deployment - street-level warfare - settlement defense - urban pacification - fortified district security

Their forces are capable of operating independently for extended periods within unstable urban environments.

The Guardians are particularly valued for their ability to: - secure populated regions - stabilize contested settlements - maintain operational control in chaotic environments

Both frontier governments and House administrators frequently rely upon the Guardians during politically unstable occupations or major civil unrest.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
Lesser Companies frequently develop operational preferences or long-standing relationships with specific Great Houses.

Unlike the Greater Companies, which attempt to preserve broader strategic neutrality, many Lesser Companies maintain: - ideological sympathies - historical alliances - favored contract networks - recurring military partnerships

This often shapes: - recruitment culture - equipment doctrine - battlefield philosophy - political reputation

Some companies are quietly regarded as unofficial extensions of House military strategy despite retaining independent Guild status.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, Lesser Mercenary Companies are generally viewed as: - elite - professional - dangerous - highly specialized - reliable

To frontier populations, a Lesser Company deployment often signals that a conflict is serious enough to require hardened professional steel.

To smaller mercenary crews, the Lesser Companies represent the first truly prestigious tier of mercenary success.

Many Free Jacks dream of someday earning a permanent banner slot within one of these legendary organizations.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability spreads throughout the Core and frontier alike, the demand for specialized mercenary warfare continues growing.

Many military observers believe the Lesser Mercenary Companies are becoming increasingly influential due to their combination of: - flexibility - professionalism - strategic specialization - operational independence

Though overshadowed politically by the Greater Companies, the Lesser Companies remain among the most dangerous and respected military forces in the modern era.

As one old Landing saying puts it:

“Anybody can hire steel. Knowing which steel to hire wins wars.”

docs.title: Mercenary Guild
docs.text: “Where words bind war.” — Guild motto

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Mercenary Regulatory Authority Headquarters Drake’s Landing Primary Function Contract Arbitration and Mercenary Oversight Political Status Neutral Institution Primary Influence Licensing, Contracts, Reputation Systems Guild Motto “Where Words Bind War.”

The Mercenary Guild is the primary regulatory institution overseeing mercenary activity throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Officially neutral and recognized by both the Star Regency and the Great Houses, the Guild exists to regulate, formalize, and contain the enormous system of private warfare that defines much of the modern era.

The Guild oversees: - mercenary licensing - contract arbitration - company registration - payment enforcement - salvage claims - reputation tracking - blacklists - operational disputes

Most legal mercenary work throughout civilized space passes through the Guild Open Board, where registered companies review available contracts, negotiate terms, and secure Guild-backed payment guarantees.

Although the Guild does not possess the military power of the Great Houses, its economic and legal authority grants it enormous influence throughout the Core.

In practice, the Guild functions as one of the primary institutions preventing mercenary warfare from descending into uncontrolled chaos.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild emerged during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

As civilization stabilized under the Star Regency, conflicts between the Great Houses increasingly shifted toward: - proxy conflicts - frontier disputes - covert operations - deniable military action

Mercenary companies expanded rapidly during this period.

Initially, the absence of regulation created widespread instability involving: - contract fraud - piracy - unauthorized escalation - civilian massacres - salvage disputes - inter-company warfare - abandoned dead claims

Several early mercenary conflicts reportedly came close to destabilizing entire frontier sectors.

Recognizing that mercenary warfare could not realistically be eliminated, the Regency and Great Houses instead supported the creation of a neutral institution capable of regulating it.

Thus the Mercenary Guild was formed.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
The Guild’s guiding principle is summarized in its motto:

“Where Words Bind War.”

Within Guild doctrine, contracts are viewed not merely as financial agreements, but as the foundation preventing civilization from collapsing into unrestricted violence.

Guild officials frequently argue that warfare itself is inevitable.

What matters is whether it remains: - regulated - enforceable - limited - survivable

To the Guild, signed terms are civilization’s final restraint upon chaos.

docs.title: Drake’s Landing
docs.text:
The Guild maintains its central headquarters on Drake’s Landing, the mercenary capital of civilized space.

The world serves as: - the largest Open Board hub in the Core - a major mech repair center - a contract exchange nexus - a recruitment hub - a salvage market - neutral negotiation territory

Thousands of pilots, crews, dock workers, mechanics, and independent companies pass through Drake’s Landing every cycle.

Guild influence is strongest there.

Many frontier pilots joke that a company is not truly legitimate until it has survived at least one contract season on Landing.

docs.title: Licensing and Registration
docs.text:
Most recognized mercenary companies operate under Guild registration.

Registered companies receive: - access to the Open Board - Guild arbitration protection - salvage recognition - payment guarantees - verified steel certification - recognized operational status

Operating outside Guild authority is technically possible, particularly in deep frontier space, but unlicensed companies are frequently viewed as pirates or raiders.

The Guild maintains extensive operational records tracking: - contract fulfillment - battlefield conduct - civilian casualty reports - salvage disputes - disciplinary actions - company reputation

These records heavily influence future contract opportunities.

docs.title: Reputation and Blacklisting
docs.text:
Within mercenary culture, reputation is often more valuable than military strength.

Reliable companies with clean Guild records frequently receive: - higher-paying contracts - priority claims - House-backed work - favorable salvage rights - long-term charter agreements

Meanwhile, companies accused of: - contract violations - piracy - civilian atrocities - employer betrayal - unauthorized escalation

may receive formal Guild sanctions or burn notices.

Severe violations can result in blacklisting.

A blacklisted company often becomes effectively unemployable within civilized space.

Many eventually drift into raiding, piracy, or grey salvage operations beyond Guild jurisdiction.

docs.title: Contract Arbitration
docs.text:
The Guild serves as the primary arbitration authority for mercenary disputes throughout the Core.

Guild arbitration courts oversee: - payment disputes - contract interpretation - salvage ownership - operational disagreements - breach accusations

When disputes occur, Guild arbitrators may issue: - arbitration locks - payment freezes - temporary suspensions - operational sanctions

Most major powers comply with Guild rulings because maintaining confidence in the mercenary system is considered strategically essential.

Without arbitration, much of frontier commerce and security would rapidly collapse.

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
Contrary to popular belief, the Guild is not a major military power.

It maintains: - station defense forces - limited patrol fleets - enforcement detachments - investigative divisions

primarily for protecting Guild infrastructure and personnel.

The Guild’s true authority derives from: - economic legitimacy - legal recognition - reputation systems - political necessity

The Great Houses tolerate and support the Guild because it prevents mercenary conflicts from escalating into direct inter-house warfare.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses make extensive use of mercenary companies.

Officially, mercenary contracts are used for: - frontier defense - anti-piracy operations - convoy escort - infrastructure security

Unofficially, many companies participate in: - proxy wars - covert operations - territorial disputes - political sabotage - quiet work

House sponsorship of mercenary companies is common, though often concealed behind intermediaries and shell contracts.

The Guild generally avoids direct involvement in House rivalries whenever possible.

Maintaining neutrality is considered essential to preserving institutional legitimacy.

docs.title: Relationship with the Regency
docs.text:
The Star Regency officially recognizes the Mercenary Guild as a neutral civil institution.

Many political observers believe the Regency quietly depends upon the Guild to contain conflicts that might otherwise escalate into open warfare between the Great Houses.

Although House Caledon occasionally pressures the Guild during periods of severe instability, the Guild has historically resisted becoming an outright arm of the Regency.

This balance between cooperation and independence remains politically delicate.

docs.title: Guild Culture
docs.text:
Guild culture tends to be highly procedural, pragmatic, and politically cautious.

Guild officials are generally less concerned with ideology than with: - enforceable agreements - operational stability - predictable conduct - conflict containment

Among mercenaries, the Guild possesses a mixed reputation.

Some pilots view it as: - civilization’s necessary referee - the protector of legitimate companies - the only thing keeping the Open Board functional

Others view it as: - bureaucratic - politically compromised - overly favorable toward large companies and House-backed outfits

Despite criticism, few mercenary companies willingly operate outside Guild systems for long.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout the Core, the Mercenary Guild is viewed as: - neutral - bureaucratic - influential - necessary - pragmatic

To frontier populations, Guild certification often represents the difference between legitimate protection and outright piracy.

To the Great Houses, the Guild serves as both a useful political instrument and a stabilizing institution.

To mercenaries themselves, the Guild is simply part of life.

As one old Landing saying goes:

“No steel rides long without a Guild seal.”

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise, the Mercenary Guild faces growing pressure from every direction.

Frontier instability, pirate activity, expanding proxy conflicts, and increasingly aggressive House politics have strained the systems established after the Great Restoration.

Many within the Guild fear that if its authority weakens too severely, the Core may once again descend into unrestricted warfare.

For this reason, the Guild continues defending the principle upon which it was founded:

Where Words Bind War.

docs.title: Mercenaries
docs.text: “The Great Houses speak of peace. Mercenaries fight the wars.” — Frontier saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Common Name Metal Mercs Primary Hub Drake’s Landing Primary Authority Mercenary Guild Primary Equipment Combat Mechs Primary Occupation Private Military Contracting Common Saying “No House Rules The Frontier.”

Mercenaries — commonly referred to throughout civilized space as Metal Mercs — are private military companies operating throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Although officially independent, mercenary organizations play a central role in the political and military balance of the modern era.

Mercenary companies are employed for: - security operations - frontier defense - anti-piracy campaigns - industrial protection - proxy conflicts - recon contracts - convoy escort - quiet work - private warfare

While the Great Houses officially prohibit open warfare against one another under the authority of the Star Regency, mercenary companies provide a means through which conflicts can occur without escalating into civilization-wide war.

As a result, mercenaries have become one of the defining institutions of modern Core society.

Throughout the frontier, many civilians view mercenary companies as both protectors and predators — armed outsiders arriving under Guild seal, contract slate in hand, and reactor smoke on the horizon.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
Mercenary warfare expanded rapidly during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

As civilization stabilized, tensions between the Great Houses increasingly shifted away from direct military confrontation and toward: - covert operations - frontier disputes - proxy conflicts - deniable warfare

Independent military companies proved ideal for these operations.

Mercenaries allowed the Houses to: - project force - secure frontier systems - influence unstable regions - pursue political objectives

without openly violating Conclave law.

Over time, mercenary culture evolved into a permanent and highly influential part of interstellar civilization.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
Most recognized mercenary companies operate under the authority of the Mercenary Guild.

The Guild functions as a semi-neutral regulatory authority responsible for: - contract arbitration - licensing - company registration - dispute mediation - reputation tracking - blacklisting - payment enforcement

The Guild officially operates independently from the Great Houses, though nearly every major power attempts to influence Guild operations to some extent.

Without Guild recognition, a company may struggle to secure legitimate contracts through the Open Board or gain access to reliable salvage markets within civilized space.

The Guild’s central headquarters are located on Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: Drake’s Landing
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing is widely regarded as the mercenary capital of the Core.

The world functions as: - a hiring center - contract exchange hub - mech repair world - training center - recruitment station - black market trade hub

Thousands of independent pilots, technicians, officers, and mercenary companies pass through Drake’s Landing every year.

The world possesses a reputation for: - opportunity - violence - corruption - ambition - independence

Throughout the frontier, many aspiring mech pilots dream of one day building their own company on Landing and earning a verified steel registration through the Guild.

docs.title: Mercenary Companies
docs.text:
Mercenary companies vary enormously in size and capability.

Some consist of only: - a handful of pilots - a transport vessel - a few aging mechs

Others rival the standing militaries of smaller planetary governments.

Many companies specialize in: - heavy assault - recon operations - frontier survival - anti-piracy warfare - urban combat - convoy protection - industrial security

A company’s reputation is often more valuable than its military strength.

Successful companies survive through: - reliability - professionalism - battlefield performance - loyalty - strategic reputation

Poor reputations can rapidly destroy a company’s access to contracts, financing, and Open Board opportunities.

docs.title: Free Jacks
docs.text:
Not all mercenaries belong to large companies or long-standing mercenary commands.

Independent operators known as Free Jacks work outside permanent affiliations, taking contracts wherever work can be found.

Some Free Jacks operate alone in single mechs.

Others form temporary crews or short-term charter groups assembled for specific contracts.

Free Jacks are common throughout: - frontier systems - salvage zones - pirate regions - unstable trade routes

Many eventually join larger companies after building enough reputation on the Open Board.

Others remain fiercely independent throughout their careers.

Within mercenary culture, Free Jacks possess a mixed reputation.

Some are respected as adaptable frontier veterans capable of surviving without House sponsorship or major company support.

Others are viewed as unreliable drifters, reactor gamblers, or walking debt barely holding their steel together long enough to survive another contract cycle.

Despite this reputation, many legendary mercenary commanders began their careers as Free Jacks operating from Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: Mechs and Mercenary Life
docs.text:
Mechs are central to mercenary culture.

Owning and maintaining combat mechs is enormously expensive, making mech ownership both prestigious and financially dangerous.

Many mercenary pilots are: - former military personnel - frontier survivors - displaced nobles - failed officers - salvagers - opportunists - ex-House pilots

A successful company may spend years accumulating: - salvage - replacement parts - weapons - reactors - transport ships - experienced crew

The destruction of even a single mech can financially cripple smaller companies.

Many independent pilots spend most of their careers riding redline financially, one bad contract or failed salvage claim away from getting dry docked permanently.

As a result, mercenary commanders often become highly pragmatic regarding: - risk - contract terms - salvage rights - reactor strain - battlefield engagement

Among veteran mercs, there is a common saying:

“A smart pilot knows when to punch out. A dead pilot keeps fighting.”

docs.title: Reputation and Morality
docs.text:
Mercenary companies possess wildly different reputations throughout the Core.

Some companies are viewed as: - honorable professionals - defenders of frontier settlements - reliable military contractors

Others are little more than heavily armed criminals operating under legal cover.

The line between: - mercenary - pirate - warlord - private army

can become dangerously thin in unstable frontier regions.

The Mercenary Guild officially condemns piracy and civilian attacks, though enforcement remains inconsistent outside major trade routes.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses employ mercenary forces to varying degrees.

Officially, mercenary contracts are used primarily for: - frontier security - anti-piracy operations - infrastructure defense

Unofficially, many companies participate in: - proxy wars - political sabotage - territorial disputes - covert military operations - quiet work

House sponsorship of mercenary companies is common, though often concealed through intermediaries and shell contracts.

The Star Regency officially discourages excessive mercenary escalation, but most political observers recognize that mercenary warfare has become one of the primary mechanisms preventing open conflict between the Great Houses themselves.

As one Guild analyst famously summarized:

“The Houses call it peace because somebody else signs the contracts.”

docs.title: Frontier Importance
docs.text:
Beyond the Core, mercenary companies often represent the closest thing frontier systems possess to organized military protection.

Many isolated settlements rely upon mercenary forces for: - pirate defense - convoy escort - local security - emergency response - law enforcement

In some regions, powerful mercenary companies effectively function as independent governments.

This has made mercenary culture deeply intertwined with frontier identity.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, mercenaries are viewed with a mixture of: - admiration - suspicion - fear - romanticism - distrust

To some, mercenaries represent freedom, independence, and opportunity beyond the rigid structures of the Great Houses.

To others, they are profiteers who prolong instability for personal gain.

Despite these criticisms, few deny the enormous influence mercenary companies now wield throughout the Core and frontier alike.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As political tensions rise throughout the Core and instability spreads across frontier space, demand for mercenary services continues to increase.

Many analysts within StarCom and the Regency fear that the growing dependence upon private military forces reflects a deeper weakening of interstellar political stability.

Others argue mercenaries have become an essential pressure valve preventing civilization from descending into open inter-house war.

Whatever the truth, one fact remains clear:

the modern age of the Core belongs as much to the Metal Mercs as it does to the Great Houses themselves.

docs.title: Fleet Structure
docs.text: Core fleets are organized around ancient capital ships supported by escorts, carriers, and logistics vessels.

docs.title: Typical Fleet Composition
docs.text:
• Battleships
  • Cruisers
  • Destroyers
  • Escort Frigates
  • Troop Carriers

Because ship losses are often irreplaceable, fleets are used cautiously.

docs.title: Military Organization
docs.text: “Steel without command is just scrap waiting to happen.” — Union field doctrine

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Primary Military Basis Hierarchical Mech Warfare Core Combat Unit Column Common Command Structure Column → Company → Ward → March Primary Combat Platform War Mechs Operational Philosophy Flexible Combined Command Common Reputation “Ancient Structure, Modern Steel”

The military structure of the Core is built upon a long tradition of hierarchical command, strategic coordination, and mechanized warfare. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

Although heavily modernized over centuries of conflict, most military systems throughout civilized space still retain organizational foundations dating back to the earliest eras of mech warfare.

Modern military doctrine emphasizes: - clear chains of command - operational flexibility - rapid deployment - mechanized coordination - scalable force structure

This system allows military forces ranging from: - small mercenary units - frontier militias - House expeditionary forces - massive interstellar commands

to coordinate effectively across enormous theaters of war.

At the center of nearly all modern military organization stands the mech.

docs.title: Structure and Hierarchy
docs.text:
Military organization throughout the Core is based upon nested command structures in which each level controls several subordinate formations. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

This hierarchy exists to provide: - battlefield clarity - operational discipline - strategic scalability - logistical coordination

The system also allows commanders to rapidly reorganize units depending upon: - terrain - mission profile - attrition - deployment limitations - political considerations

Although every major faction maintains unique command traditions and rank titles, the underlying organizational structure remains broadly recognizable throughout civilized space.

docs.title: The Column
docs.text:
The fundamental operational unit of modern mech warfare is the Column. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

A standard Column consists of: - four mechs - one commanding officer - supporting technical staff - logistical attachments

Columns are typically commanded by a captain or equivalent junior field officer depending upon factional doctrine.

The Column is designed to function as a highly flexible battlefield element capable of: - reconnaissance - assault operations - patrol work - urban combat - defensive action - independent deployment

Because of its versatility, the Column forms the foundation of nearly all military operations throughout the Core.

Among mech pilots, the phrase “holding the line with four steel” is a common reference to the importance of the Column structure.

docs.title: Reinforced Columns
docs.text:
In high-risk or specialized operations, militaries may deploy Reinforced Columns consisting of: - six mechs - occasionally eight mechs

These formations are especially common among: - mercenary companies - frontier militias - assault specialists - irregular warfare groups

Reinforced Columns sacrifice some mobility and logistical efficiency in exchange for increased tactical flexibility and battlefield endurance.

docs.title: Companies
docs.text:
Three Columns are typically organized into a Company. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

A standard Company fields: - twelve mechs - dedicated logistical support - tactical coordination staff - maintenance personnel

Companies represent the smallest formation capable of sustained independent combat operations.

Many mercenary companies derive their name from this organizational level, though actual mercenary force sizes vary enormously.

Reinforced Companies may field: - sixteen to twenty mechs - additional logistics support - specialist units

depending upon operational doctrine.

docs.title: Wards
docs.text:
Several Companies combine to form a Ward. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

Wards generally consist of: - forty to fifty mechs - command support staff - integrated logistics networks - coordinated battlefield doctrine

Wards serve as the backbone of most large-scale military operations.

They possess enough force projection capability to: - secure territory - defend strategic regions - conduct major offensives - hold urban sectors

Many military historians consider the Ward the smallest truly strategic mech formation.

docs.title: Marches
docs.text:
Several Wards combine into a March. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

Marches usually field: - one hundred to two hundred mechs - extensive logistical infrastructure - mobile command elements - aerospace support attachments

Marches are capable of: - sustained planetary campaigns - regional occupation - major breakthrough operations - prolonged attritional warfare

In frontier conflicts, the deployment of a full March is often viewed as evidence that a conflict has escalated into a major military crisis.

docs.title: Brigades, Divisions, and Corps
docs.text:
At the upper levels of military organization, formations scale into truly enormous mechanized armies. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

These formations include:

docs.title: Brigade
docs.text:
• 1,000–2,000 mechs
  • 7–10 Marches

docs.title: Division
docs.text:
• approximately 10,000 mechs
  • 4–5 Brigades

docs.title: Corps
docs.text:
• approximately 50,000–75,000 mechs
  • 5–7 Divisions

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
• approximately 200,000–300,000 mechs
  • 3–6 Corps

These immense formations require: - planetary-scale logistics - fleet coordination - industrial support - strategic command infrastructure

Few modern conflicts involve full Command-level deployments due to their staggering economic and logistical requirements.

docs.title: The Scale of Modern Warfare
docs.text:
Despite the enormous numbers associated with upper-level formations, true Command-scale warfare is exceedingly rare in the modern Core.

The cost of: - mech maintenance - transport logistics - reactor fuel - ammunition production - fleet support

makes prolonged large-scale warfare extraordinarily difficult.

As a result, many modern conflicts revolve around: - limited offensives - proxy warfare - mercenary intervention - strategic raids - territorial disputes

rather than total war.

docs.title: Faction-Specific Hierarchies
docs.text: Although military organization remains broadly standardized, each major faction utilizes distinct command titles rooted in its own history and culture. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
The Imperium maintains some of the oldest surviving military traditions in civilized space.

Imperium titles are believed to predate even the Old Empire itself.

Their hierarchy emphasizes: - ancient duty - guardianship - continuity - martial tradition

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
The Union uses highly modernized military terminology emphasizing: - operational clarity - efficiency - battlefield coordination

Due to the Union’s military influence, Union command terminology is especially common among mercenary organizations.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
The Sovereignty blends military command with aristocratic tradition.

Many officer titles possess noble or ceremonial connotations reflecting the faction’s dynastic culture.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
Protectorate command structure emphasizes: - defense - guardianship - territorial stewardship

Their titles often reflect protective or martial symbolism.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
Corporate military structure blends traditional military command with administrative and industrial terminology.

Corporate command philosophy emphasizes: - coordination - systems management - operational integration

docs.title: Comparative Command Titles
docs.text:
Unit Mercenary Union Sovereignty Protectorate Imperium Corporate Column Lieutenant Lieutenant Leftenant Armsman Sentinel Lieutenant Company Captain Captain Captain Praetor Vigilant Captain Ward Major Major Major Warden Warden Major March Colonel Colonel Duke Commandant Guardian Marshal Brigade Brigadier General Brigadier General Brigadier General Spartan General General Viceroy Second General Division General General General General General General Corps Varies Field Marshal Lord Marshal Grand Marshal Subcommander Flag Marshal Command * First Commander Lord Commander Oberst Commander High Commander Commodore Supreme Commander * High Minister Archduke / Archon Herzog Imperator Prodirector

No known mercenary organization has ever reached true Supreme Command scale. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

docs.title: Mercenary Adaptations
docs.text:
Mercenary organizations frequently modify traditional military structures to suit operational requirements. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

Common adaptations include: - reinforced columns - irregular company sizes - modular command structures - temporary battlefield formations - contract-specific task groups

Greater Mercenary Companies often develop highly sophisticated command systems rivaling those of the Great Houses themselves.

Meanwhile, smaller mercenary outfits may operate with extremely informal hierarchies built more around: - reputation - battlefield experience - pilot seniority - contractual authority

than formal military tradition.

docs.title: Combined Arms Warfare
docs.text:
Although mech formations dominate modern military doctrine, successful campaigns require extensive coordination between: - mechs - infantry - aerospace forces - artillery - fleet assets - logistics divisions

Modern battlefield doctrine increasingly emphasizes integrated combined-arms operations rather than pure mech superiority alone.

Even the most powerful mech formations remain vulnerable without adequate support infrastructure.

docs.title: Cultural Importance
docs.text:
Military rank and organizational structure remain deeply embedded within the identity of the Core.

Across civilized space, military terminology influences: - politics - mercenary culture - civilian administration - industrial organization - social prestige

Veteran officers often carry enormous political influence long after active service ends.

Many House leaders themselves possess military backgrounds or ceremonial military titles.

Throughout much of the Core, military hierarchy is viewed not merely as organizational necessity —

but as one of the foundational structures preserving civilization itself.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability rises throughout the Core and frontier regions alike, military organizations continue adapting to the realities of modern warfare.

Advancements in: - mech coordination - neural networking - battlefield communication - electronic warfare - logistics management

are gradually reshaping traditional command structures.

Even so, the ancient hierarchy remains remarkably resilient.

From the smallest four-mech Column to the vast Commands that marshal hundreds of thousands of war machines, the military structure of the Core continues to reflect a civilization built upon organized steel, disciplined command, and the constant shadow of war.

docs.title: Military Ranks
docs.text: Military ranks vary between the Great Houses, though most follow broadly similar command structures inherited from older Core traditions.

docs.title: Common Ranks
docs.text:
• Recruit
  • Trooper
  • Sergeant
  • Lieutenant
  • Captain
  • Major
  • Colonel
  • General

docs.title: Arthur Payne
docs.text:
Arthur Payne is the current military ruler of the Helios Sovereignty and one of the most powerful political figures within the modern Core. Though the Sovereignty formally remains under the authority of the Star Regent, many political observers consider Payne to be the true operational power behind the throne during the Regent’s later years.

Born into House Payne during a period of political stagnation within the Sovereignty, Arthur gained prominence through military service rather than inheritance alone. Historical accounts describe his early career as unusually distinguished for a noble of his status, with Payne earning a reputation for discipline, administrative competence, and strategic ruthlessness during a series of frontier security campaigns and internal military reforms.

Arthur’s rise accelerated following the gradual decline in the Regent’s health. Appointed as Voice of the Regent approximately a decade ago, Payne assumed increasing responsibility over military policy, interstellar trade security, diplomatic enforcement, and internal administration throughout the Sovereignty.

Since taking office, Payne has overseen extensive reforms to the Sovereignty’s military readiness, trade infrastructure, and frontier defense systems. Supporters credit him with stabilizing commerce, reducing piracy along major Sovereignty routes, and restoring political cohesion during a period of growing instability across the Core.

Critics however accuse Payne of consolidating excessive authority around himself and ruling through fear, intimidation, and political pressure. Rival factions frequently portray him as authoritarian, expansionist, and dangerously ambitious. His uncompromising style has earned him both admiration and deep resentment among allies and adversaries alike.

Payne is widely regarded as an exceptionally capable administrator. Unlike many aristocrats of the Sovereignty, he is known for maintaining direct involvement in military logistics, economic planning, and political operations. Reports from within the Sovereignty consistently describe him as intensely demanding of both himself and those serving under him.

Publicly, Arthur Payne projects an image of rigid discipline, calculated restraint, and unwavering confidence. He is known for blunt rhetoric, little tolerance for incompetence, and a belief that stability can only be maintained through strength and decisive leadership.

Despite his reputation, Payne’s relationship with the aging Star Regent appears unusually complex. While many citizens view Payne as the dominant figure within the Sovereignty’s government, numerous political analysts have noted that the Regent himself has occasionally acted to moderate or restrain Payne’s harsher policies. This dynamic has fueled persistent speculation regarding the future balance of power within the Sovereignty once the current Regent dies.

Payne is also known to maintain a close relationship with the Regent’s son and heir, serving in many respects as both political mentor and military advisor. Nevertheless rumors of rivalry and tension between the two persist throughout court circles, particularly regarding the question of whether Payne will retain his position and influence following the eventual succession.

Outside the Sovereignty, Arthur Payne is viewed with a mixture of respect and apprehension. Even political rivals generally acknowledge his competence and strategic ability. Across the Core he is widely regarded as one of the few leaders capable of maintaining large-scale political order during an increasingly unstable era.

To supporters he represents discipline, strength, and stability. To critics he embodies the growing concentration of power within the Sovereignty and the dangers posed by increasingly authoritarian rule.

Regardless of perspective, few dispute Arthur Payne’s influence upon modern Core politics.

docs.title: Deckard Noddack
docs.text:
Deckard Noddack is the current executive leader of House Noddack and one of the most influential figures within the Orion Corporate. Known for his reserved demeanor and intense interest in ancient Imperial technology, Noddack has overseen a significant expansion of Corporate research and defensive operations in recent years.

Born into a prominent scientific and political family within the Corporate, Deckard was educated extensively in engineering, history, and systems analysis. From an early age he developed a strong fascination with the remnants of the Golden Age and the Old Empire and the technologies left behind by earlier civilizations.

Prior to assuming leadership of House Noddack, Deckard participated in multiple archaeological and scientific expeditions involving abandoned Imperial facilities and derelict research installations. Several of these expeditions resulted in important technological recoveries that strengthened the Corporate’s research capabilities.

Under Noddack’s leadership the Orion Corporate has increasingly shifted focus toward technological self-sufficiency, advanced research initiatives, and the restoration of ancient infrastructure. This inward focus has generated growing suspicion among rival Houses, many of whom fear the Corporate may be attempting to monopolize lost technologies from the Imperial era.

Political rivals frequently accuse Noddack of prioritizing technological advancement over diplomacy and regional cooperation. Supporters however argue that the Corporate’s scientific leadership remains essential to maintaining the long-term stability and advancement of civilization within the Core.

Deckard himself remains a highly private figure. Unlike many leaders of the Great Houses, he rarely engages in populist rhetoric or large public spectacles. Most of his public appearances are associated with scientific conferences, technological initiatives, or Corporate security announcements.

Within the Core, Noddack has become strongly associated with the growing competition over ancient Imperial ruins and forgotten technologies. Many observers believe the Orion Corporate under his leadership is positioning itself as the leading authority on pre-collapse scientific knowledge and advanced engineering.

Though admired by many within scientific and academic circles, Deckard Noddack remains a controversial figure among rival powers wary of the Corporate’s growing technological influence.

docs.title: Jada Lytherius
docs.text:
Jada Lytherius is the current ruler of the Omnisphere Imperium and one of the most influential political figures in the modern Core. Known widely by titles such as “The Savior” and, more controversially, “The Viper,” Jada rose to power during the devastating civil conflict known as the Free Suns Rebellion.

Jada was the third child born to Empress Seraphina Lytherius, the expansionist ruler often remembered as the “Last Great Noble.” Following Seraphina’s unexpected death, leadership of the Imperium passed not to Jada but to her eldest daughter, Empress Valeria Lytherius.

Valeria attempted to rule as a reformer during a period of growing unrest within the Imperium. Her efforts to negotiate with dissident worlds and reduce political tensions were widely viewed as ineffective, particularly as separatist movements began spreading through the outer Imperial territories. Matters worsened when several worlds formally declared independence under the banner of the Free Suns rebellion.

Rumors long persisted that foreign powers, particularly elements within the Confederate Vanguard Union, covertly supplied or encouraged the rebellion, though no definitive evidence has ever been publicly presented.

As the rebellion escalated, Valeria’s authority rapidly deteriorated. She was eventually assassinated under circumstances that remain officially unresolved. Leadership then passed to her younger brother, Cassian Lytherius, whose rise to power proved deeply controversial within many Imperial circles due to the Imperium’s strong dynastic preference for female succession.

At the time of these events, Jada herself was living away from the Imperial capital. Unlike her siblings, she had not been groomed for political leadership and instead devoted much of her life to the study of philosophy, art, and Imperial history. She was also already the mother of two sons, both still in their youth when the rebellion began.

When summoned back to the capital by her brother following Valeria’s death, Jada instead fled into rebel-held territory alongside her sons. Rebel leaders quickly embraced her presence, believing that support from a legitimate member of House Lytherius would strengthen the political legitimacy of their movement.

Jada’s role within the rebellion remains the subject of intense historical debate. Contemporary accounts suggest she was initially underestimated by both rebel commanders and loyalist officials alike, who viewed her as politically inexperienced and easily manipulated. Over time however she emerged as one of the rebellion’s most influential symbolic figures.

As the war dragged on, public opinion increasingly shifted against Cassian Lytherius. Civilian massacres, failed military operations, and growing unrest within loyalist territories severely damaged his reputation. Supporters of Jada portray this period as proof of her ability to inspire hope amid chaos, while critics argue that she skillfully exploited the conflict to consolidate political power around herself.

Eventually Jada was captured by loyalist forces and returned to the Imperial capital. Shortly afterward, Cassian Lytherius was assassinated by members of his own inner circle under circumstances that remain heavily disputed. Jada was subsequently released and declared the rebellion formally ended.

The final collapse of the Free Suns movement occurred soon afterward during negotiations between remaining rebel leaders and Jada’s supporters. Numerous prominent rebel commanders were killed during the confrontation, effectively ending organized resistance and allowing Jada to assume undisputed control of the Imperium.

Since ascending to the throne, Jada Lytherius has focused heavily on restoring Imperial stability, rebuilding damaged worlds, and strengthening central authority. Her supporters view her as the ruler who preserved the Imperium from collapse during its darkest hour. Critics however accuse her government of fostering a culture of fear, political manipulation, and ruthless centralization beneath its carefully crafted image of unity and renewal.

Jada’s relationships with foreign powers remain notably strained. She has repeatedly criticized both the Confederate Vanguard Union and the Conclave for their alleged roles during the rebellion. Relations with the Star Regent in particular remain politically sensitive due to the Regent’s refusal to directly intervene during the Imperial civil conflict.

Today Jada Lytherius remains one of the most admired and feared individuals in human space: a ruler celebrated as a savior by some and regarded as a dangerously calculating political survivor by others.

docs.title: Victor Hawkins
docs.text:
Victor Hawkins is the current military leader of the Confederate Vanguard Union and one of the most respected strategists within the modern Core. Known widely by the nickname “The Grey Wolf,” Hawkins is recognized for combining military pragmatism with diplomatic skill during several major border crises involving the Union.

Born into House Hawkins, Victor was raised within the heavily militarized culture of the Union during a period of increasing regional instability. Historical records describe his upbringing as highly disciplined, with much of his early education focused on strategy, leadership, and statecraft under the supervision of senior Union military officials.

Victor first gained widespread recognition during his early military career while serving in covert operations along contested frontier regions. Though many details of these operations remain classified, his reputation for careful negotiation and unconventional strategy emerged during this period.

His rise within the Union military hierarchy was rapid, aided by several successful campaigns involving anti-piracy operations, border stabilization efforts, and strategic defense initiatives. Supporters within the Union portray him as a symbol of discipline, competence, and national unity during an increasingly uncertain era.

Unlike many previous Union leaders, Victor is often regarded as more politically flexible and diplomatically capable than purely ideological. Foreign observers have frequently noted his willingness to negotiate with rival powers when doing so serves Union interests.

Public perception of Hawkins throughout the Core is sharply divided. Within the Union he is widely admired as a stabilizing figure capable of preserving security and order. Outside Union territory however, critics accuse him of expanding covert intelligence operations, supporting proxy conflicts, and pursuing increasingly aggressive regional policies under the justification of national defense.

Despite his military reputation, Victor maintains a relatively restrained public image compared to many contemporary political leaders. He rarely appears in entertainment media and is known for avoiding excessive displays of wealth or ceremony.

Political analysts frequently describe Hawkins as representative of the Union itself: disciplined, pragmatic, highly strategic, and willing to make difficult decisions in pursuit of long-term stability.

docs.title: Drake's Landing
docs.text: “Every merc gets a shot. No promises on a second one.” — Dockside saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
