Region Within Starcrest Protectorate borders Primary Role Neutral mercenary hub Major Authority Mercenary Guild Primary Industry Contracts, training, recruitment, and mercenary operations Cultural Identity Independence, strategy, martial prowess Common Reputation “The Mercenary Capital of the Core”

Drake’s Landing is one of the most important mercenary worlds in the Core.

Although located within the borders of the Starcrest Protectorate, the world is recognized as neutral ground for mercenary activity, contract negotiation, recruitment, and inter-faction dealings.

Its neutrality has allowed it to become a central gathering point for:

  • mercenary companies
  • independent soldiers of fortune
  • employers
  • Guild brokers
  • contract negotiators
  • military trainers
  • House intermediaries

Drake’s Landing is celebrated for its culture of independence, strategy, and martial skill. It is both sanctuary and proving ground: a place where mercenary companies are founded, tested, broken, rebuilt, and remembered.

docs.title: Modern Significance
docs.text:
The modern use of Drake’s Landing as a neutral mercenary hub has transformed it into one of the busiest military-commercial centers in civilized space.

Its open ports and inclusive contract culture allow individuals from many backgrounds to gather, exchange skills, recruit talent, and arrange complex operations.

The world supports:

  • contract exchanges
  • military training
  • recruitment halls
  • neutral negotiations
  • Guild arbitration
  • operational planning
  • mercenary networking

Unlike the polished institutions of the Great Houses, Drake’s Landing is practical, crowded, and direct. A commander’s name matters less than their record. A company’s banner matters less than whether its pilots return with their steel intact.

For many Free Jacks and young mercenary crews, Drake’s Landing is the first place where ambition becomes a real contract.

docs.title: Historical Background
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing owes its name and legacy to the legendary mercenary commander Drake.

According to traditional accounts, Drake was hired to defend a strategically insignificant planet against an anticipated invasion. The assignment was initially viewed as a routine defensive contract.

When the invading force arrived, however, it proved vastly larger and more dangerous than expected.

Drake’s forces were badly outnumbered, and by conventional military calculation the planet should have fallen quickly.

Instead, Drake turned the campaign into one of the most famous mercenary actions in Core history.

docs.title: The Legend of Drake
docs.text:
Facing overwhelming odds, Drake used the planet’s rugged terrain to deny the enemy a clean victory.

His unit relied upon:

  • guerrilla tactics
  • ambushes
  • hit-and-run strikes
  • psychological warfare
  • strategic retreats
  • terrain denial

Rather than attempting to defeat the invaders in open battle, Drake forced them into a long and exhausting campaign.

Every advance cost them time, supplies, morale, and lives.

Each victory became pyrrhic.

By delaying the invasion force and disrupting its command structure, Drake bought enough time for his employer to gather and deploy relief forces.

When those reinforcements finally arrived, the invaders were already exhausted, demoralized, and out of position.

The relief force routed them.

In recognition of Drake’s leadership and the decisive role he played in saving the planet, his employer granted him stewardship over the world.

From that point forward, the planet became known as Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: From Stronghold to Mercenary World
docs.text:
With Drake’s mercenary unit now based on the planet, Drake’s Landing gained a reputation as an impregnable stronghold.

Its association with Drake’s victory deterred future invasions and attracted other mercenary forces seeking a secure operational base.

Over time, the world transformed from a defended military outpost into a growing center of mercenary culture.

Mercenary companies came to Drake’s Landing for:

  • shelter
  • repair
  • recruitment
  • contracts
  • training
  • alliance-building

The largest mercenary units operating from the world eventually helped form the Mercenary Guild, further cementing Drake’s Landing as the heart of mercenary activity in the Core.

As its influence grew, Drake’s Landing was eventually granted independence, becoming the neutral mercenary hub it is known as today.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild is central to Drake’s Landing’s modern identity.

The Guild regulates contract arbitration, company registration, reputation records, blacklisting, and mercenary law.

On Drake’s Landing, Guild authority is stronger than anywhere else in civilized space.

The planet’s contract halls and Open Board exchanges are among the busiest in the Core, drawing:

  • Great House agents
  • frontier governors
  • corporate employers
  • independent contractors
  • Free Jacks
  • veteran companies
  • new crews seeking their first Guild seal

Although Drake’s Landing remains famously rough, its Guild presence prevents the world from collapsing fully into lawless private warfare.

docs.title: Myth vs. Reality
docs.text:
The precise truth of Drake’s life is uncertain. The names of his employer, the invading force, and many of the campaign’s details have been obscured by generations of retelling.

Some versions describe Drake as a noble commander. Others depict him as a ruthless contract fighter who happened to choose the right battlefield. Some even portray him in a negative light, suggesting he might not have been a legitimate mercenary at all but instead an invader himself, who attacked the planet known as Drake's Landing in an effort to seize it. In this depiction, after a relentless campaign of harrassment he was given Drake's Landing in a capitulation and in order to have him cease his prolonged guerilla attacks on the planet.

Whatever the truth, historians generally agree that the core of the story reflects real events, but many details have become legend. Among the people of Drake’s Landing, this distinction matters little. The legend itself has become part of the world’s identity. Drake’s Landing does not merely preserve the memory of Drake’s victory; it lives inside it.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing is defined by mercenary culture.

The world values:

  • independence
  • tactical intelligence
  • courage
  • reputation
  • practical skill
  • martial competence

Its society is unusually open by Core standards. Former soldiers, Free Jacks, House veterans, failed nobles, salvagers, pilots, mechanics, and contract brokers all mix in the same districts.

Landing culture is also deeply transactional.

Names matter. Records matter more.

A company that keeps its contracts, protects its crew, and brings back salvage earns respect quickly.

A company that breaks terms, abandons clients, or gets pilots killed for nothing may find itself burned from the Open Board before the next contract cycle.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout the Core, Drake’s Landing is viewed as:

  • independent
  • dangerous
  • strategic
  • mercenary
  • culturally distinct
  • politically useful

To the Great Houses, it is a necessary neutral ground where violence can be negotiated before it becomes open war.

To the Mercenary Guild, it is the institutional center of regulated private warfare.

To Free Jacks, it is a place to become someone.

To frontier worlds, it is often the source of the only protection they can afford.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise, Drake’s Landing grows increasingly important to the political balance of the Core.

Its Open Boards, Guild courts, training grounds, and negotiation halls shape the flow of mercenary force throughout civilized space.

Many StarCom analysts regard Drake’s Landing as one of the essential pressure valves preventing House rivalries from escalating into open war.

For now, Drake’s Landing remains what it has always been:

a world built from a legend, sustained by contracts, and defended by those who choose steel over banners.

docs.title: The Frontier
docs.text:
Beyond the Fringe Worlds lies the Frontier, a vast and poorly documented region of human space extending far beyond the effective authority of the Great Houses. Unlike the Core and Fringe, the Frontier possesses no unified political structure, stable trade network, or commonly accepted territorial boundaries. Much of the region remains only partially charted, while countless systems are known solely through fragmented reports, outdated navigation data, and rumor.

Knowledge of the Frontier within the Core is limited. Most information originates from StarCom exploration records, long-range traders, salvage expeditions, mercenary companies, and occasional diplomatic contact with isolated worlds. Even this information is often incomplete or decades out of date due to the immense distances involved and the unreliability of interstellar communication beyond established Core routes.

Human settlements within the Frontier vary dramatically in size, technology, and culture. Some worlds maintain relatively stable societies and trade periodically with the Core through StarCom-mediated routes. Others exist as isolated colonies, mining settlements, tribal states, pirate enclaves, or heavily militarized fortress worlds. In many regions technological capabilities are significantly lower than within the Core, with limited industrial infrastructure and poor access to advanced manufacturing.

Travel through the Frontier is dangerous and unpredictable. Navigation routes are poorly maintained, many systems lack reliable jump infrastructure, and emergency support is often nonexistent. Ships operating deep within the Frontier must frequently rely on self-sufficiency and improvised repairs for extended periods.

Pirates, raiders, smugglers, and criminal organizations flourish throughout many Frontier regions. Numerous hidden outposts and clandestine settlements exist beyond the reach of House authority. Bounty hunters, mercenary companies, prospectors, and fortune seekers are also common, drawn by the possibility of abandoned Imperial ruins, untapped resources, or lost technology.

The Frontier occupies an unusual place within Core culture. To many civilians it represents a realm of danger, mystery, and opportunity. Frontier stories are popular subjects within entertainment media, often portraying distant systems as lawless regions populated by pirates, warlords, rogue mercenaries, and forgotten civilizations. While many of these stories are exaggerated, the lack of reliable information allows myth and rumor to flourish.

Despite its lawlessness, the Frontier is not entirely uncivilized. StarCom maintains formal contact with a number of distant worlds and trading partners deep beyond the Fringe. Some remote systems possess stable governments, functioning industries, and long-standing local traditions independent of the Great Houses. These worlds may see Core politics as distant concerns with little relevance to daily life.

The deeper regions of the Frontier remain almost entirely unknown to the Core. Exploration beyond established trade corridors becomes increasingly rare, and many expeditions simply vanish without explanation. Stories persist of lost colonies, ancient Imperial outposts, hidden pirate kingdoms, and isolated civilizations untouched by modern Core politics.

For the Great Houses, the Frontier represents both risk and opportunity. Though difficult to control directly, the region contains valuable resources, potential trade routes, salvage sites, and strategic locations. At the same time its vast distances and fragmented political landscape make large-scale military operations extremely difficult.

To most citizens of the Core however, the Frontier remains less a clearly defined region than an idea: the edge of civilization, where the authority of the Houses fades and humanity disappears into the darkness between the stars.

docs.title: Titan Prime
docs.text: “On Titan Prime, a pilot can become immortal… or explode before the second round.” — Arena broadcast saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Location Within Starcrest Protectorate Borders Political Status Neutral World Primary Function Gladiatorial Mech Combat Major Industry Broadcasting and Arena Sports Economic Driver Betting and Sponsorships Common Reputation “The Gladiator World”

Titan Prime is the most famous gladiatorial world in the Core and the undisputed center of professional mech combat sports.

Known throughout civilized space as The Gladiator World, Titan Prime attracts: - mercenary pilots - House champions - Free Jacks - aspiring rookies - corporate sponsors - gamblers - spectators

from across the Core and frontier alike.

The planet’s colossal arenas host high-profile mech battles broadcast to billions across known space.

To many citizens of the Core, Titan Prime represents: - glory - fame - violence - celebrity - spectacle - wealth

The world has become synonymous with mech combat entertainment itself.

docs.title: The Modern Games
docs.text:
Modern arena combat on Titan Prime is treated simultaneously as: - sport - entertainment - military exhibition - cultural spectacle

Pilots compete in a wide variety of formats including: - duel matches - lance battles - team tournaments - endurance campaigns - elimination brackets - live-fire exhibition events

The games attract everyone from: - decorated veteran pilots - House-sponsored champions - mercenary celebrities - desperate Free Jacks hoping for recognition

Victorious pilots can earn: - enormous sponsorship deals - Guild recognition - celebrity status - House recruitment offers - lucrative contract opportunities

For many struggling mercenaries, a successful Titan Prime performance can permanently transform a career.

docs.title: Arena Culture
docs.text:
Titan Prime’s arenas are not merely battlegrounds.

They are stages.

Pilots are treated as: - athletes - celebrities - military icons - entertainment figures

Arena broadcasts dominate large portions of Core media culture.

Famous pilots frequently endorse: - mech manufacturers - reactor brands - weapons systems - Guild services - combat stimulants - luxury products

Some celebrity mech pilots possess fan followings rivaling major political figures.

Entire mech chassis configurations occasionally become fashionable after successful arena seasons.

Throughout the Core, countless young pilots dream of one day hearing their callsign announced before a packed Titan arena.

docs.title: Economy and Infrastructure
docs.text:
The economic impact of the games on Titan Prime is enormous.

The world supports massive industries centered around: - broadcasting - betting - sponsorships - mech repair - mech manufacturing - pilot training - hospitality - entertainment

Titan Prime contains some of the most advanced: - training facilities - mech servicing docks - combat simulators - arena engineering systems

in civilized space.

The planet’s economy is deeply tied to the continued popularity of the games.

Entire sectors of the Core economy are influenced by: - betting markets - sponsor networks - arena licensing - broadcast rights

docs.title: Betting and Gambling
docs.text:
Betting culture is deeply woven into Titan Prime society.

Fortunes are regularly: - won - lost - manipulated - destroyed

on the outcome of major matches.

Professional gamblers, data analysts, Guild brokers, and House-backed financial interests all participate heavily in arena wagering markets.

Some matches generate betting volumes rivaling major industrial trade exchanges.

This gambling culture contributes significantly to Titan Prime’s: - excitement - corruption - celebrity obsession - political influence

Among arena regulars, there is a common saying:

“The house always wins. The pilots just survive long enough to spend it.”

docs.title: Historical Shadows
docs.text:
Despite the glamour of the modern games, Titan Prime’s origins remain deeply controversial.

Many historians note that the original coliseums were not built for entertainment.

They were built for punishment.

Long before the modern era of professional arena combat, Titan Prime’s arenas served as brutal theaters where: - political prisoners - prisoners of war - criminals - slaves

were forced to fight, often to the death.

docs.title: The Dark Beginnings
docs.text:
Historical records indicate that early arena organizers treated combatants as commodities rather than athletes.

Slavers and criminal syndicates reportedly purchased pilots directly and forced them into combat servitude.

Although a small number of successful fighters achieved fame and relative luxury, many remained effectively imprisoned within the arena system.

Public executions and lethal combat spectacles became normalized forms of mass entertainment.

Some historians argue that the modern games continue to romanticize this darker legacy.

Others maintain the current era represents a complete cultural transformation.

The subject remains politically sensitive on Titan Prime itself.

docs.title: The Ophidian Occupation
docs.text:
Ironically, the collapse of the original arena system occurred during the Ophidian Occupation.

Although the Occupation is remembered across the Core as an age of suffering and collapse, many historians view it as the turning point that ended the old gladiatorial slave system on Titan Prime.

When the games eventually returned decades later, Core society had changed dramatically.

Public executions, slave combat, and forced arena servitude had largely fallen out of favor throughout civilized space.

The reborn games were restructured as professional competitive sport rather than state-sanctioned execution.

This transformation permanently reshaped Titan Prime’s identity.

docs.title: The Modern Debate
docs.text:
Despite reforms, debate surrounding Titan Prime remains intense.

Critics argue the games still exploit: - impoverished mercenaries - desperate Free Jacks - debt-ridden pilots - psychologically damaged veterans

Supporters counter that modern arena pilots: - compete voluntarily - earn enormous rewards - achieve social mobility - inspire billions

Many pilots themselves openly embrace the risks.

Within mercenary culture, surviving Titan Prime is often viewed as proof of elite skill.

docs.title: The Great Houses and Titan Prime
docs.text:
All five Great Houses maintain a strong interest in Titan Prime.

The arenas serve as: - recruitment grounds - propaganda platforms - technology showcases - cultural influence centers

House-sponsored pilots frequently become major celebrities.

Political rivalries occasionally spill indirectly into arena competition, though official regulations prohibit overt inter-house warfare within Titan Prime events.

Unofficially, many observers suspect the arenas are used for: - covert negotiations - influence operations - sponsorship wars - intelligence gathering

beneath the spectacle of public entertainment.

docs.title: Mech Combat as Entertainment
docs.text:
Titan Prime helped transform mech warfare into mass entertainment across the Core.

Arena broadcasts have heavily influenced: - popular culture - mech aesthetics - military propaganda - sports culture - civilian perceptions of warfare

Many critics within StarCom and the Regency quietly worry that generations of citizens raised on arena broadcasts increasingly view warfare itself as spectacle rather than tragedy.

Others argue the games provide a safe outlet for humanity’s fascination with violence and competition.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, Titan Prime is viewed as: - glamorous - dangerous - legendary - excessive - corrupt - culturally influential

To mercenaries, it represents opportunity.

To the Great Houses, it is a useful political and cultural instrument.

To critics, it is proof that the Core remains obsessed with violence beneath its veneer of civilization.

To billions of ordinary citizens, however, Titan Prime remains simple:

the greatest show in the galaxy.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
Titan Prime’s influence continues to grow with every passing decade.

As mech combat culture expands across the Core, the arenas increasingly shape: - public opinion - military celebrity - mech design trends - sponsorship economies - mercenary recruitment

Some analysts believe Titan Prime now exerts more cultural influence than entire planetary governments.

Whether viewed as noble sport, exploitative spectacle, or civilized gladiatorial warfare, one truth remains undeniable:

when the arena lights ignite on Titan Prime, the entire Core watches.

docs.title: Cities of the Core
docs.text:
The cities of the Core are among the largest and most densely populated human settlements in known space. Built atop layers of older Imperial infrastructure accumulated across centuries, many Core cities stretch for hundreds of kilometers and contain populations numbering in the tens or even hundreds of millions.

Modern Core cities are typically composed of multiple historical layers. At their centers stand the active urban districts of the modern age: dense commercial sectors, government centers, industrial zones, residential arcologies, and transportation hubs maintained by the Great Houses and local planetary authorities. Beneath and beyond these active districts however lie older regions dating back to the Empire, many of which now stand abandoned or only partially inhabited.

Towering arcologies dominate the skylines of many wealthy Core worlds. These immense self-contained structures house residential sectors, commercial districts, industrial facilities, transit systems, and public services within single interconnected megastructures. Some arcologies contain populations equivalent to entire cities on their own.

Infrastructure within the Core is heavily dependent upon ancient Imperial systems still maintained centuries after their construction. Automated transit networks, subterranean maglev systems, orbital elevators, atmospheric processors, and industrial fabrication complexes remain essential to daily life. Though modern engineers can maintain and repair much of this infrastructure, much of its original design and construction methods are only partially understood.

Social inequality is common within many cities of the Core. Wealthy districts are often clean, heavily secured, and technologically sophisticated, populated by political elites, corporate executives, military officers, and influential merchant families. Lower industrial sectors and labor districts are typically more crowded and economically unstable, particularly on manufacturing and refinery worlds.

Beneath many major cities lie vast underlevels and abandoned sectors collectively referred to by numerous local names such as the Undercity, Lower Zones, Dead Levels, or Old Districts. These areas often contain forgotten transit tunnels, collapsed industrial facilities, abandoned residential sectors, and sealed military infrastructure left behind from earlier periods of Imperial expansion.

Some abandoned districts have become home to criminal organizations, smugglers, salvage crews, undocumented populations, and black-market industries operating beyond effective government oversight. Local authorities frequently tolerate such activity so long as it remains contained within lower sectors and does not threaten the stability of the upper city.

Security within major Core cities is extensive. Surveillance systems, automated checkpoints, local security forces, and House military patrols are common in politically important urban centers. Despite this, organized crime, political corruption, sabotage, and espionage remain widespread beneath the surface of civilized society.

Daily life within the cities of the Core is shaped by both modern civilization and the lingering presence of the Empire. Citizens may commute through transit tunnels older than their governments, live within structures constructed centuries earlier, or work alongside machinery whose original manufacturers no longer exist.

For many citizens the cities of the Core represent the height of human civilization: safe, prosperous, technologically advanced, and culturally vibrant. At the same time they also serve as reminders of humanity’s gradual decline. No modern government could fully recreate the immense urban systems inherited from the Empire, and much of the ancient cityscape now exists as silent ruins surrounding the surviving centers of civilization.

docs.title: CoreNet
docs.text:
CoreNet is the primary interstellar communications and media network used throughout civilized human space. Maintained through a vast system of relay stations, orbital transmitters, planetary hubs, and StarCom communication infrastructure, CoreNet allows the rapid distribution of news, propaganda, entertainment, military reports, financial data, and civilian communications across much of the Core.

Although often described as a single unified network, CoreNet is in reality a fragmented collection of regional systems maintained by governments, corporations, StarCom installations, and private communication providers. The central Core worlds possess the most advanced and reliable access, while communication quality and transmission speed degrade significantly farther into the Fringe and Frontier.

For most civilians CoreNet serves as the primary source of information about events beyond their homeworlds. News broadcasts, political commentary, military updates, entertainment programming, and House propaganda are consumed daily across the Core. Most major governments maintain extensive media divisions dedicated to shaping public perception through carefully managed reporting and patriotic broadcasts.

Political propaganda is a major feature of CoreNet culture. The Great Houses regularly accuse one another of corruption, instability, aggression, or economic exploitation through official broadcasts and media campaigns. Frontier incidents, pirate attacks, border disputes, and military operations are frequently framed in ways favorable to each House’s political interests.

Mercenary companies also maintain a substantial presence across the network. Popular mercenary commanders, mech pilots, and military units often become celebrities through interviews, combat footage, sponsorship deals, and public recruitment campaigns. Arena combat broadcasts and military exhibitions are among the most widely viewed entertainment programs within many regions of the Core.

Despite its importance, CoreNet is not fully centralized or universally accessible. Transmission delays become increasingly severe farther from the Core due to the limitations of relay infrastructure and the dangers of maintaining communication routes through unstable frontier regions. Some distant systems may receive updates weeks or months after events occur within the central Core.

Censorship and information control vary considerably between regions and political powers. Certain governments heavily regulate local media and restrict outside broadcasts, while others permit relatively open information exchange. During periods of political crisis or military conflict, emergency restrictions and communication blackouts are common.

The Frontier remains only loosely connected to CoreNet. Many distant settlements possess limited or outdated communication infrastructure, while some isolated worlds operate entirely independent local networks. Information arriving from deep frontier space is often fragmented, unreliable, or heavily distorted by rumor and myth.

StarCom plays a critical role in maintaining long-distance communication infrastructure throughout human space. Many remote relay stations and frontier communication hubs depend entirely on StarCom maintenance and logistical support. As a result, StarCom possesses enormous influence over the flow of information between the Core, Fringe, and Frontier.

Beyond simple communication, CoreNet functions as one of the primary cultural bonds holding the Core together. Citizens separated by vast interstellar distances still consume many of the same news broadcasts, propaganda campaigns, sporting events, and entertainment programs. In this way CoreNet helps sustain a shared sense of civilization across a fragmented and declining human empire.

docs.title: The Deep Fringe
docs.text:
Beyond the settled Core and frontier systems lies the Deep Fringe — isolated regions of space with weak governance, poor communications, and countless dangers.

The Deep Fringe is home to raiders, isolated colonies, mercenary enclaves, and forgotten relics of earlier ages.

docs.title: The Fringe Worlds
docs.text:
The Fringe Worlds are independent planetary states located along the outer edge of the Core. Though culturally and economically connected to the Core in varying degrees, these worlds are not formally governed by any of the Great Houses. Fringe governments range from stable republics and military protectorates to authoritarian regimes, mining syndicates, and merchant coalitions.

Unlike the Great Houses, Fringe Worlds generally lack the industrial capacity and logistical infrastructure required to maintain large interstellar fleets. Most possess only small defensive spacecraft intended for local patrol, customs enforcement, or orbital defense. Their military power is instead concentrated on the defense of individual planets through planetary militias, mech forces, fixed defenses, and localized aerospace units.

While no single Fringe World could rival the military power of a Great House, many are nevertheless formidable within their own systems. Invasion of a major Fringe World would often prove costly and politically difficult, especially given the limited strategic value of many frontier regions. As a result the Great Houses typically prefer diplomacy, trade agreements, economic pressure, or indirect influence rather than direct military conquest.

Life within the Fringe is generally harsher and less predictable than within the central Core. Infrastructure is often older and less reliable, while economic conditions vary dramatically from world to world. Frontier farming colonies, mining settlements, refinery towns, and isolated industrial outposts are common. Civilian populations frequently rely on local militias and mercenary contracts for protection against raiders and pirates.

Piracy is significantly more common within Fringe space. Numerous pirate clans, raider groups, smuggling networks, and criminal organizations operate from hidden bases on remote moons, asteroid fields, and isolated systems. Trade convoys traveling through the Fringe often require armed escorts, while many smaller settlements maintain extensive underground shelters and defensive fortifications.

Mercenary companies play an important role throughout the Fringe. Many independent worlds lack the manpower to maintain large standing armies and instead rely on mercenary units to supplement local defense forces. Successful mercenary commanders can become highly influential figures on Fringe Worlds, occasionally acting as military advisors, governors, or even political leaders.

The Fringe also serves as a gateway to the Frontier beyond. Trade routes, exploration expeditions, salvage operations, and StarCom relay stations extend outward from Fringe space into increasingly isolated and poorly documented regions. Information beyond the Fringe becomes fragmented and unreliable, with many systems remaining only partially charted.

Culturally, Fringe populations often view themselves as more independent and self-reliant than the populations of the central Core. While many worlds maintain strong economic and cultural ties to the Great Houses, distrust of centralized authority is common. Frontier traditions, local militias, survivalism, and planetary identity tend to play a larger role in public life than within the highly urbanized Core worlds.

To many citizens of the Core, the Fringe represents both danger and opportunity. It is a region associated with fortune seekers, mercenary companies, smugglers, explorers, bounty hunters, and pioneers. Though less stable than the central Core, the Fringe remains one of the few regions of human space where individuals and organizations can still rapidly rise to power and influence.

docs.title: Life in the Core
docs.text:
The Core is the heart of civilized human space and the center of political, economic, and cultural life. It occupies the territory once ruled by the ancient Empire, a civilization far larger and more populous than the modern Core. Though the great cities of the Core rival those of the Imperial age in safety and prosperity, much of the old Empire remains abandoned. Across even the wealthiest worlds, forgotten districts, sealed arcologies, and ruined industrial sectors stand as reminders of a greater age.

For most civilians, life in the Core is stable and relatively secure. The average citizen is far removed from the battlefields and frontier raids often depicted on the CoreNet. Millions work ordinary jobs as laborers, mechanics, engineers, administrators, merchants, medical personnel, and technicians. While standards of living vary between worlds and social classes, the central Core worlds are generally prosperous and orderly.

Daily life differs dramatically depending on location. Citizens of wealthy central worlds often live in towering urban centers supported by advanced infrastructure inherited from the Empire. High-speed transit systems, automated manufacturing, and vast commercial districts dominate these worlds. Outside the largest population centers however, signs of decline are common. Entire districts may stand abandoned for centuries, while old transit tunnels and industrial sectors remain sealed or forgotten beneath modern cities.

In the outer regions of the Core, life becomes harsher. Agricultural colonies, mining settlements, and frontier industrial worlds often require difficult manual labor and constant maintenance simply to survive. Equipment failures, supply shortages, and pirate activity are more common farther from the central worlds. In many regions mercenary companies supplement local House forces in defending civilian populations from raiders and criminal organizations.

Mercenaries occupy a unique place within Core culture. To many civilians they are viewed as larger-than-life figures, somewhere between soldiers, celebrities, and gladiators. Famous mercenary commanders and mech pilots are often featured in entertainment broadcasts, recruitment advertisements, and news coverage. Arena combat, military tournaments, and mech exhibitions attract enormous audiences across the Core. While many admire mercenaries for their skill and independence, others view them as dangerous opportunists motivated solely by profit.

Despite the relative safety of the Core, political tensions between the Great Houses are ever present. News broadcasts regularly feature reports of border incidents, trade disputes, sabotage accusations, pirate raids, and military exercises. Most citizens however experience these conflicts indirectly through propaganda, entertainment, and economic fluctuations rather than open warfare.

Beyond the authority of the Great Houses lie the Fringe Worlds and the distant Frontier. To many Core citizens these regions are viewed with a mixture of fascination and suspicion. Stories of pirates, frontier warlords, ancient ruins, and unexplored systems remain popular subjects throughout Core media and entertainment.

Though the modern Core remains powerful and civilized, it exists in the shadow of the Empire that came before. Humanity survives not through expansion, but through preservation. Much of daily life within the Core revolves around maintaining the immense infrastructure, technology, and institutions inherited from earlier ages.

docs.title: Mercenaries
docs.text:
Mercenaries, colloquially known as “Metal Mercs,” play an indispensable role in the military and political machinations throughout the Core. These skilled pilots and their mech units are a common sight, offering their services to the highest bidder.

Mercenaries are hired by all of the great houses of the Core to supplement their armed forces, execute military operations, and sometimes even to serve as the primary force in conflicts where deniability is key.

docs.title: Mercenary Operations
docs.text:
Mercenaries come with the expectation of providing their own mechs, ensuring they are battle-ready for whatever their contracts demand. Compensation for their services typically includes a mix of direct payment and salvage rights, allowing successful mercenaries to profit and improve their arsenals with each mission.

This arrangement benefits the great houses as well, providing them a way to engage in conflicts indirectly, maintaining a veil of plausible deniability and mitigating the risk of attracting direct retaliation or the disfavor of the Star Regent.

docs.title: Drake's Landing: The Mercenary Hub
docs.text:
Drake's Landing is renowned as the central hub for mercenary activity within the Core. Named after the legendary mercenary commander Drake, who famously defended the planet against overwhelming odds, Drake's Landing evolved from a fortified stronghold to a vibrant world where mercenaries from across the galaxy converge.

The planet's status as a neutral ground makes it an ideal location for mercenary units to recruit, resupply, and negotiate contracts. With a rich history steeped in mercenary lore, Drake's Landing embodies the spirit of the mercenary life: freedom, honor, and the pursuit of victory.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
Overseeing the vast network of mercenary activity is the Mercenary Guild, a governing body established to regulate and support the mercenary community across the Core. The guild ensures that mercenary contracts are honored, providing a level of oversight and protection against exploitative practices.

Their motto, “Where words bind war,” reflects the foundational belief in the sanctity of the mercenary contract, emphasizing that agreements between employers and mercenaries are not just transactions but sacred bonds that must be respected by all parties.

The guild's influence extends to protecting even those mercenary units not formally bound to it, advocating for fair treatment and equitable contracts throughout the sector. By upholding these standards, the Mercenary Guild has become a pillar of the mercenary profession.

docs.title: Mercenary Companies
docs.text:
In the Core, major mercenary companies are categorized into Greater and Lesser companies.

Greater Mercenary Companies are the largest units, often functioning as massive independent armies with their own fleets and bases across several worlds. They operate under fully established command hierarchies that resemble governmental or council-like structures, with no single individual owning or commanding the entire organization.

Lesser Mercenary Companies are still substantial but usually operate on a smaller scale. They generally maintain a single established base, command several starships, and consist of a few divisions. Leadership in these companies often centers around a single individual, making them more nimble but also tightly dependent on their commander's vision and capabilities.

Below these, countless smaller mercenary units exist, often referred to as the Minor Mercenary Companies. Varying in size from just a few columns to those spanning entire wards or marches, the overwhelming majority of mercenary companies in the Core fall into this category.

These companies are the most likely outfits to be commissioned by local leaders and magistrates, especially near the Fringe, who may be facing smaller-scale issues such as security details or pirate and bandit protection.

docs.title: Mercenary Culture
docs.text:
Mercenaries occupy a unique and highly visible role within Core society. Though technically private military contractors, many mercenary companies possess enormous public recognition and cultural influence across civilized space. To much of the civilian population, mercenary pilots and commanders are viewed less as ordinary soldiers and more as legendary warriors, celebrities, and symbols of independence.

The public fascination with mercenaries stems partly from the rarity of open warfare within the Core. Large-scale conflicts between the Great Houses are uncommon due to the immense political and economic risks involved. As a result, most civilians experience warfare indirectly through news broadcasts, propaganda reports, arena competitions, recruitment campaigns, and heavily publicized mercenary operations.

Successful mercenary companies often cultivate highly recognizable public identities. Company insignias, mech paint schemes, famous pilots, and battlefield reputations are widely known throughout the Core. Popular mercenary commanders frequently appear in interviews, military documentaries, advertisements, and entertainment broadcasts. Some companies maintain dedicated media divisions responsible for public relations and propaganda.

Arena combat and military exhibitions are a major source of public interest across many worlds. While true combat remains dangerous and politically sensitive, organized mech duels, training demonstrations, and sponsored competitions attract enormous audiences. These events serve both as entertainment and as recruitment tools for House militaries and mercenary organizations alike.

Public attitudes toward mercenaries are mixed. Many civilians admire mercenary pilots as heroic and independent figures willing to operate in dangerous frontier regions beyond the safety of the central Core. Others view mercenary companies as opportunistic and morally questionable organizations motivated primarily by profit rather than loyalty or duty.

In the Fringe Worlds mercenaries are often viewed far more pragmatically. Frontier settlements frequently rely on mercenary protection against pirates, raiders, and hostile militias. In many isolated systems mercenary companies may provide security, disaster relief, convoy protection, and even local governance where formal state authority is weak or absent.

The relationship between mercenary companies and the Great Houses is complex. While the Houses publicly emphasize the importance of their own military forces, nearly all rely heavily on mercenary units for deniable operations, frontier defense, anti-piracy campaigns, and proxy conflicts. Competition for elite mercenary contracts can become a significant element of inter-House politics.

Mercenary life itself is highly romanticized within Core media. Recruitment advertisements and entertainment programs frequently portray mercenary pilots as symbols of freedom, adventure, and personal ambition. Young civilians throughout the Core often dream of joining famous companies and piloting mechs on distant frontier worlds.

The reality of mercenary service however is often far harsher. Casualties remain common, frontier deployments can last years, and many smaller companies survive only through dangerous salvage operations and low-paying security contracts. Numerous mercenary groups disappear entirely during pirate raids, failed campaigns, or frontier expeditions far from the Core.

Despite these dangers, mercenaries remain deeply embedded within the culture and mythology of human space. Across the Core and Fringe alike, the image of the independent mech pilot continues to symbolize humanity’s ability to survive amid the instability and decline of the modern age.

docs.title: Pirates and Raiders
docs.text:
Piracy remains one of the most persistent threats throughout the Fringe Worlds and Frontier regions. While large-scale naval warfare between the Great Houses is relatively rare, smaller conflicts involving pirate fleets, raider bands, smugglers, and criminal syndicates occur constantly beyond the heavily patrolled Core systems.

Pirate groups vary enormously in size and organization. Some consist of little more than a handful of lightly armed ships targeting isolated cargo convoys and frontier settlements. Others operate as highly organized criminal enterprises controlling hidden bases, smuggling networks, salvage operations, and black-market trade routes across multiple systems.

Most pirate activity occurs within the Fringe, where the authority of the Great Houses weakens and civilian shipping routes become more difficult to protect. Merchant convoys traveling through frontier systems frequently hire mercenary escorts, while many independent settlements maintain extensive local defenses against raids.

Pirate bases are commonly hidden on remote moons, asteroid belts, abandoned mining colonies, or forgotten Imperial facilities. Many such outposts are intentionally located far from major jump routes to avoid detection by House patrols or StarCom monitoring stations. Some larger pirate enclaves function as full criminal settlements complete with repair docks, marketplaces, refineries, gambling halls, and black-market trade hubs.

Raiders differ somewhat from traditional pirates in both organization and objectives. Whereas pirates generally focus on smuggling, theft, and profit, raider groups are often more militarized and destructive. Raider attacks may target civilian settlements directly, seizing supplies, fuel, industrial equipment, or prisoners before withdrawing into poorly charted frontier regions.

Numerous criminal organizations operate within pirate-controlled territories. Smugglers traffic in illegal weapons, military technology, narcotics, forged identification systems, and stolen industrial goods. Salvage gangs frequently strip abandoned Imperial ruins or battlefields for valuable components and weapons systems.

Relations between pirate groups and the Great Houses are often more complicated than officially acknowledged. Though all major governments publicly condemn piracy, House intelligence agencies have at times been accused of covertly supporting certain pirate organizations for political or strategic purposes. Pirates may also serve as intermediaries for smuggling operations, deniable attacks, or black-market trade networks connected to larger political interests.

Mercenary companies frequently conduct anti-piracy operations throughout the Fringe. Escort missions, bounty contracts, base assaults, and convoy defense operations represent a major source of employment for smaller mercenary units. Successful pirate hunters can become highly respected figures within frontier regions, while notorious pirate commanders may achieve infamy throughout the Core.

For ordinary civilians living near the Fringe, piracy is often viewed as an unavoidable reality of frontier life. Many settlements maintain emergency bunkers, planetary militia forces, and warning systems specifically designed to respond to pirate attacks. Convoy delays, supply shortages, and security alerts are common occurrences on remote worlds.

Within Core media and popular culture, pirates occupy a contradictory role. Official propaganda portrays them as violent criminals threatening civilized space, while entertainment programs often romanticize pirate life as rebellious and adventurous. Frontier folklore similarly contains countless stories of hidden treasure fleets, rogue admirals, lost pirate kingdoms, and legendary raider captains operating beyond the reach of House authority.

Despite ongoing military campaigns and security operations, piracy persists because of the immense scale of human space itself. The Great Houses simply cannot patrol every trade route, moon, asteroid field, and isolated colony scattered across the Fringe and Frontier.

docs.title: Ruins of the Empire
docs.text:
Scattered across the Core are the remnants of the ancient Empire, vast abandoned regions left behind following centuries of decline, depopulation, economic collapse, and political fragmentation. Though the modern Core remains highly civilized and technologically advanced, much of humanity now occupies only a fraction of the infrastructure once maintained during the height of Imperial rule.

On many worlds, modern cities stand beside or atop far older urban centers dating back to the Empire. Massive abandoned districts, sealed transit systems, forgotten industrial sectors, and derelict arcologies are common even on wealthy Core planets. Entire metropolitan regions may stand empty for hundreds of kilometers beyond the populated zones of modern cities.

The Empire possessed a far larger population than the modern Core and maintained extensive interstellar infrastructure across thousands of systems. During its height, planetary populations reached levels that many modern worlds could no longer sustain following the collapse of centralized authority and the decline of interstellar trade networks. Over time, populations consolidated around defensible and economically viable urban centers while countless peripheral settlements were abandoned.

Many ruins remain dangerous and poorly understood. Automated defense systems, unstable infrastructure, toxic industrial contamination, and unexplored subterranean sectors continue to pose hazards centuries after abandonment. Some ancient transit systems and utility networks still function autonomously despite no longer being fully understood by modern engineers.

Imperial ruins are especially common beneath the largest cities of the Core. Vast underground levels, abandoned maglev tunnels, forgotten military bunkers, and sealed industrial complexes often lie beneath modern population centers. In some cities entire lower districts have been declared permanently off-limits due to structural instability, criminal occupation, or unexplained system failures.

Salvage operations within Imperial ruins represent a major industry throughout the Core and Fringe alike. Lost technology, industrial components, military hardware, and archival data recovered from ancient facilities can command enormous value. Many mercenary companies supplement their income through salvage contracts, escort duties, and ruin security operations.

The Great Houses frequently conduct classified expeditions into major Imperial sites, particularly former research installations, shipyards, and military facilities. Rumors persist of hidden Imperial weapons caches, sealed artificial intelligences, lost colonies, and experimental technologies buried within forgotten sectors of the old Empire.

For ordinary citizens the ruins of the Empire are both mundane and mythic. Most Core civilians live among Imperial infrastructure in some form, using transportation systems, reactors, or industrial machinery whose origins long predate modern civilization. At the same time the deeper ruins remain objects of fascination, superstition, and fear. Stories of hidden vaults, lost riches, rogue defense systems, and vanished expeditions are common throughout Core media and frontier folklore.

The existence of the ruins serves as a constant reminder that modern civilization survives in the shadow of a greater age. Humanity did not inherit an expanding empire, but the remnants of one already fallen.

docs.title: Technology
docs.text: Technology in the Core is defined by powerful relic industries, ancient engineering traditions, and the machines that allow civilization to survive across interstellar space.

docs.title: Major Technology Entries
docs.text:
• Mechs
  • Weapons
  • Space Warfare
  • Jump Technology

docs.title: Union Mechs
docs.text:
Union mech forces combine widely available Core designs with heavy industrial frames built for mass deployment, durability, and sustained mechanized assault.

This roster includes both the common Core designs available to all major factions and the signature designs most closely associated with the Confederate Vanguard Union.

docs.title: Roster Summary
docs.text: Mech Model Hornet HN-120 Lynx LNX-40E Outrider OR-640 Yeoman YM-10F Lineman LM-30C Lancer LNC-220 Longstrike LG-210 Champion CH-70K Bulldog BD-770 Marksman MK-350 Gladiator GL-882 Kingsguard KG-950 Raptor RPT-1R Vagabond VG-7H Slugger SLG-1A Alpha Wolf AWLF-5K Crossfire CRF-0S Firebrand FR-3N Dragoon DGN-8S Maverick MAV-8K Jackman JCK-77 Patriot PTR-007 Hammerhead HMR-4T Challenger CHL-4R Brigadier BRG-88R Bulwark BLW-5K Sword Breaker SBRK-7R Punisher PNR-004 Gallimagus GLM-8G Liberator LBR-9D

docs.title: Hornet HN-120
docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Lynx LNX-40E
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Outrider OR-640
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Raptor RPT-1R
docs.text: Developed as a fast-response raider during the Union’s early frontier wars, the Raptor sacrifices heavy armor for speed and strike capability. Its rocket pods and light lasers made it ideal for harassing supply lines and crippling softer targets before heavier Union assets arrived. Though lightly protected, pilots trusted its agility and ferocity—earning the name Raptor for its swift, predatory strikes.

docs.title: Vagabond VG-7H
docs.text: The Vagabond was born of necessity during the Exile, when scattered Union units required a versatile scout and skirmisher. Outfitted with jump jets and a mixed ballistic-energy loadout, it excels in rough terrain where heavier mechs cannot tread. Its name reflects both its wandering origins and its reputation as a survivor, roaming far from supply lines and returning with kills to prove its worth.

docs.title: Slugger SLG-1A
docs.text: Designed for brutal city fighting during the Restoration, the Slugger carries a heavy energy cannon backed by close-support lasers. Slow but unyielding, it was built to clear enemy strongpoints building by building. The name Slugger is both a nod to its hammering firepower and the Union’s reputation for winning through attrition, no matter how long the fight drags on.

docs.title: Alpha Wolf AWLF-5K
docs.text: The Alpha Wolf represents the Union’s doctrine of coordinated pack warfare. Armed with paired autoguns, lasers, and missiles, it was designed to anchor wolfpack formations that overwhelmed isolated foes through relentless firepower. Feared for its ability to tear through both armor and morale, the Alpha Wolf earned its name by always leading the kill.

docs.title: Crossfire CRF-0S
docs.text: Born from Union doctrine that no enemy should leave the field unscathed, the Crossfire bristles with overlapping weapon arcs—Vulkan cannon, rockets, lasers, and autogun. It was intended for siege and suppression, saturating kill zones until nothing remained. Pilots often joked that standing in its line of fire was like being caught between two armies—hence the name Crossfire.

docs.title: Firebrand FR-3N
docs.text: The Firebrand’s origin lies in the Union’s scorched-earth campaigns. Armed with an array of Scorchers supported by lasers and autoguns, it was built to terrorize entrenched enemies, burning them from cover and leaving nothing but ash. The Union embraced the mech’s fiery symbolism, fielding it as both weapon and warning: resistance would be answered with flames.

docs.title: Dragoon DGN-8S
docs.text: The Dragoon was designed as a flexible heavy line unit, able to hold or advance with equal confidence. Combining a heavy energy cannon, vulkan, and defensive lasers, it proved especially valuable in prolonged campaigns where reliability outweighed elegance. Its name recalls the mounted infantry of old—soldiers who could fight wherever they were needed, a tradition the Union holds dear.

docs.title: Maverick MAV-8K
docs.text: The Maverick was developed for commanders who demanded independence from supply trains. Its rockets and high energy cannon made it a devastating ambush platform, while its jump jets allowed it to strike from unexpected angles. The name Maverick honors both its battlefield role and the defiant Union spirit of breaking from convention to seize victory.

docs.title: Jackman JCK-77
docs.text: The Jackman was built during the urban reclamations, where Union forces needed a close-range brawler to clear streets and hold chokepoints. Armed with paired vulkans and heavy lasers, it is devastating in knife-fight ranges. The name reflects the laborers who “jackhammered” the Union’s cities back into shape, embodying relentless industrial grit turned to war.

docs.title: Patriot PTR-007
docs.text: The Patriot is perhaps the most symbolic of Union mechs, a walking fortress embodying their doctrine of sacrifice and resilience. With thick armor, broad weapon coverage, and missile support, it became the backbone of ceremonial as well as battlefield formations. Its name was no accident—the Union intended it as a rallying banner, a machine that stood unbroken while others fell.

docs.title: Hammerhead HMR-4T
docs.text: Developed for shipboard and port assaults, the Hammerhead mounts twin heavy energy cannons backed by autoguns and lasers. Its name derives from its brutal frontal assault style, “hammering” through defenses like the predator of the sea. Pilots often describe it as inelegant but unstoppable—exactly the qualities the Union prizes.

docs.title: Challenger CHL-4R
docs.text: The Challenger was created during the Union’s dueling trials, where mechs were fielded to prove dominance over rival Houses. Mixing rockets, autoguns, and lasers, it was designed for versatility in single combat as well as battlefield challenges. Though less specialized than some designs, its name and legacy are steeped in Union pride: to challenge is to overcome.

docs.title: Brigadier BRG-88R
docs.text: Built to lead batteries of missile carriers, the Brigadier became the cornerstone of Union siege doctrine. Capable of drowning entrenched foes in waves of rockets and missiles, it earned a reputation for ending battles before they truly began. The name Brigadier reflects its command role—always at the center of Union formations, directing fire and breaking lines.

docs.title: Bulwark BLW-5K
docs.text: Designed as a defensive anchor, the Bulwark carries massive armor and layered weaponry suited for holding ground against superior numbers. In campaigns where the Union was forced to defend its hard-won territories, the Bulwark became both shield and hammer. Its name speaks for itself: immovable, unshakable, a wall of steel.

docs.title: Sword Breaker SBRK-7R
docs.text: The Sword Breaker was engineered specifically to counter the dueling champions of rival Houses. With heavy vulkans and precision medium lasers, it excelled at stripping weapons from enemy mechs and leaving them powerless. Its name is both literal and symbolic—the Union’s message that no foreign blade, however sharp, could pierce their resolve.

docs.title: Punisher PNR-004
docs.text: The Punisher was born from campaigns of retribution. With a heavy energy cannon, ultra vulkan, and missile support, it was built to chase down and annihilate those who dared defy Union authority. Slow but merciless, the Punisher lives up to its name, serving as executioner on the battlefield where no mercy is given.

docs.title: Gallimagus GLM-8G
docs.text: The Gallimagus is an ancient design, said to descend from pre-Exile Empire prototypes. Mounting an unprecedented four heavy energy cannons, it was rarely produced due to cost, but every unit became a battlefield legend. Its name, drawn from old Union sagas, evokes a monstrous beast of war—rare, mythical, and terrifying in its wrath.

docs.title: Liberator LBR-9D
docs.text: Developed during the Long Exile by remnants of the fallen Empire, the Liberator was designed to spearhead counteroffensives and break enemy sieges. It played a decisive role in the Great Restoration, leading the charge to reclaim the Core worlds. Adopted as the Union’s premiere assault mech, it endures as both battlefield powerhouse and enduring symbol of liberation.

docs.title: Sovereignty Mechs
docs.text:
Sovereignty mech forces combine common Core platforms with prestigious, high-quality designs associated with aristocratic command, battlefield coordination, and visible military refinement.

This roster includes both the common Core designs available to all major factions and the signature designs most closely associated with the Helios Sovereignty.

docs.title: Roster Summary
docs.text: Mech Model Hornet HN-120 Lynx LNX-40E Outrider OR-640 Yeoman YM-10F Lineman LM-30C Lancer LNC-220 Longstrike LG-210 Champion CH-70K Bulldog BD-770 Marksman MK-350 Gladiator GL-882 Kingsguard KG-950 Messenger MSG-1R Icarus ICS-3G Pegasus PGS-H Sentry SN-21 Warden WRD-N1 Lamplighter LLT-6Y Legionnaire LGN-A Paladin PLDN-00C Herald HRL-6D Protector PRO-T1 Dark Knight DK-2R Excalibur EX-3R Lionheart LHR-5T Lancelot LNC-10T Great Sword SWRD-001 Colossus COL-U5 Monarch MON-A7 Olympian OLYM-D

docs.title: Hornet HN-120
docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Lynx LNX-40E
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Outrider OR-640
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Yeoman YM-10F
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Lineman LM-30C
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Lancer LNC-220
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Longstrike LG-210
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Champion CH-70K
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Bulldog BD-770
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Marksman MK-350
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Gladiator GL-882
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Kingsguard KG-950
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Messenger MSG-1R
docs.text: A swift harasser and scout, the Messenger relies on jump jets and missile bursts to unsettle lighter foes. Its role is to deliver a clear message on the battlefield—that Sovereignty forces are advancing, and their arrival is inevitable.

docs.title: Icarus ICS-3G
docs.text: The Icarus embodies Sovereignty daring and hubris—fast, jump-capable, and armed with rockets for dramatic strikes. Though lightly armored, its theatrics earned it glory in countless tournaments. Pilots know the cautionary tale of its name, but many still fly it recklessly, chasing the sun of renown.

docs.title: Pegasus PGS-H
docs.text: Inspired by myth, the Pegasus was designed as an aerial duelist, its jump jets giving it a graceful leap few others could match. Bristling with light and medium lasers, it dances around heavier foes, striking with elegance. In parades as in battle, the Pegasus is prized as a symbol of Sovereignty’s radiant majesty.

docs.title: Sentry SN-21
docs.text: The Sentry was built for watchful dominance, its long-range high energy cannon and missile banks intended to hold borders in clear view. It lacks mobility, but its very presence is a deterrent, projecting Sovereignty vigilance. Many noble estates still keep a Sentry posted at their gates, a statue of steel as much as a guardian.

docs.title: Warden WRD-N1
docs.text: Where the Sentry watches, the Warden intervenes. Agile for its size, it was built to guard noble households and convoy routes. Its heavy laser and missiles strike with authority, while its name reinforces Sovereignty propaganda: every subject is safe under noble protection—so long as they remain loyal.

docs.title: Lamplighter LLT-6Y
docs.text: A mech named for illumination, the Lamplighter was created to pierce darkness both literal and symbolic. Its long-range energy cannon and array of lasers allow it to light up a battlefield from afar. To the Sovereignty, deploying Lamplighters is an act of enlightenment, burning away shadows of rebellion with radiant fire.

docs.title: Legionnaire LGN-A
docs.text: The Legionnaire was mass-produced during Sovereignty campaigns to enforce order in the Core. Fitted with a medium MAC, missiles, and lasers, it is a versatile soldier’s machine. The name recalls the legions of ancient empires—disciplined, unstoppable, and loyal to the Sovereignty above all else.

docs.title: Paladin PLDN-00C
docs.text: A mech wrapped in religious and chivalric symbolism, the Paladin was designed to embody the Sovereignty’s holy mandate. Rockets for righteous fury, lasers for guiding light, and a MAC to deliver judgment. In battle, it is as much a sermon as a weapon, reminding allies and enemies alike of the Sovereignty’s divine right to rule.

docs.title: Herald HRL-6D
docs.text: Where nobles feared assassination or insurrection, Heralds were deployed. Heavy armor and powerful missile barrages made them excellent protectors of VIPs and sacred sites. To this day, Sovereignty rulers keep Heralds in their personal retinues, their name a constant reminder of paternal care—and veiled threat.

docs.title: Protector PRO-T1
docs.text: The Protector was created as a battlefield shield for Sovereignty banners. Mixing heavy lasers with rockets and missiles, it can blunt enemy advances while rallying allies. Noble houses often decorate Protectors with gilded heraldry, ensuring that their role as both bulwark and symbol is unmistakable.

docs.title: Dark Knight DK-2R
docs.text: The Dark Knight carries an aura of dread deliberately cultivated by Sovereignty engineers. Long-range energy weapons paired with a heavy laser give it punishing reach, while its sinister name and black-lacquered armor turn it into an icon of fear. Where the Dark Knight walks, rebellion tends to wither.

docs.title: Excalibur EX-3R
docs.text: Few names carry more weight than Excalibur, and the Sovereignty bestowed it upon this duelist’s chassis. Armed with MACs, lasers, and rockets, it was designed for decisive, theatrical victories. Excaliburs often lead tournament fields, their pilots draped in noble colors, striking as much for honor as for conquest.

docs.title: Lionheart LHR-5T
docs.text: The Lionheart epitomizes Sovereignty nobility: powerful, courageous, and unflinching. With heavy lasers, rockets, and long-range cannons, it dominates battlefields while bearing crests of golden lions. More than a weapon, it is a living coat of arms, proof that Sovereignty bloodlines rule by strength and valor.

docs.title: Lancelot LNC-10T
docs.text: The Lancelot was built to combine elegance with battlefield dominance. Its MAC and missile systems give it both reach and crushing power, while its lasers keep foes at bay. No Sovereignty parade is complete without a Lancelot striding at the fore, the paragon of chivalric warfare.

docs.title: Great Sword SWRD-001
docs.text: The Great Sword is the Sovereignty’s answer to the Union’s brute heavies: less subtle, more theatrical. Quad MACs and paired heavy lasers strike with the force of a descending blade. Its very silhouette evokes a weapon raised for the killing blow, a message that Sovereignty justice is swift and merciless.

docs.title: Colossus COL-U5
docs.text: Massive and imposing, the Colossus is a walking fortress designed to awe as much as destroy. Mounting a heavy MAC alongside multiple lasers, it crushes resistance under overwhelming firepower. Wherever a Colossus is deployed, it is as much a proclamation as an army—Sovereignty power manifest in steel.

docs.title: Monarch MON-A7
docs.text: The Monarch was never just a war machine—it was designed as a throne of iron for Sovereignty lords. With missile arrays and heavy lasers, it wields devastating power while carrying the regalia of its house. To see a Monarch on the field is to witness Sovereignty authority embodied, its very name leaving no doubt of who rules.

docs.title: Olympian OLYM-D
docs.text: The Olympian is the pinnacle of Sovereignty design philosophy: mythic in name, majestic in presence, and terrible in power. With MACs, long-range cannons, missiles, and lasers, it commands every battlefield range. Sovereignty propaganda hails it as proof of their divine heritage—an Olympian god made metal, standing above all mortal challengers.

docs.title: Jump Drive Technology
docs.text:
Jump drives are the foundation of interstellar civilization within the Core. Without them, communication, trade, military operations, and political authority across human space would collapse almost immediately. Despite their importance however, very few individuals fully understand how jump drives actually function.

Modern civilization relies upon mathematical and technological principles inherited from the long ago, whose original discoveries remain only partially understood. While contemporary scientists and navigators can operate and maintain jump systems reliably, the deeper theoretical foundations behind faster-than-light travel remain among the greatest mysteries in human history. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

docs.title: Hypercomplex Mathematics
docs.text:
At the center of all jump technology are Hypercomplex Numbers, an advanced mathematical framework far beyond conventional real, imaginary, or complex mathematics.

Hypercomplex systems allow navigators to model spacetime geometries that would otherwise be impossible to calculate. These equations form the basis for: - faster-than-light travel - gravitational prediction - jump corridor stabilization - paradox resolution - temporal correction - high-dimensional navigation

Modern computers are incapable of deriving these equations independently. Instead, navigational systems rely heavily upon ancient calculation libraries, inherited formula sets, and partially understood computational heuristics. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

Most navigators and engineers treat Hypercomplex mathematics as an applied science rather than a fully understood field of study.

docs.title: Chaotic Numbers
docs.text:
One of the most important components of jump mathematics is the use of Chaotic Numbers.

Chaotic Numbers encode entire dynamic systems within recursive mathematical structures, allowing navigators to model unstable gravitational environments that would normally be impossible to predict accurately. They are especially critical in solving variations of the Three-Body Problem, where the interactions of multiple stars, planets, moons, and gravity wells produce unpredictable trajectories. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

Rather than calculating a single path through space, Chaotic Numbers allow jump systems to account for entire fields of possible outcomes simultaneously. This permits ships to safely navigate regions of extreme gravitational complexity while avoiding catastrophic instability.

Modern navigators often describe Chaotic calculations as less "predicting" the future and more "constraining" the range of acceptable realities through which a ship may travel.

docs.title: Paradoxical Numbers
docs.text:
Paradoxical Numbers form the second major pillar of jump mathematics.

Where Chaotic Numbers manage unstable systems, Paradoxical Numbers address the causality violations inherent to faster-than-light travel itself. These equations allow spacetime to fold in ways that appear to violate conventional temporal order while maintaining broader physical consistency. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
